entries.question: What is the McStud Estimating Tool?
entries.answer:
The McStud Estimating Tool (MET) is a comprehensive collision repair estimating system with **10 specialized tabs**:

**1. SOP List** - Standard Operating Procedures (17 inputs)
**2. Part Operations** - Parts and panels (161 inputs)
**3. Cover Car** - Masking and protection (8 inputs)
**4. Body Operations** - Sheet metal work (25 inputs)
**5. Refinish Operations** - Paint and finish (11 inputs)
**6. Mechanical Operations** - Suspension/drivetrain (34 inputs)
**7. SRS Operations** - Airbag/safety systems (9 inputs)
**8. Total Loss Charges** - Salvage fees (10 inputs)
**9. Body On Frame** - Truck/SUV frames (4 inputs)
**10. Stolen Recovery** - Theft-related ops (11 inputs)

Total: **290 input controls** that auto-calculate labor, price, and refinish hours.

entries.question: What is the SOP List tab?
entries.answer:
The **SOP List** (Standard Operating Procedures) tab contains **17 inputs** organized into sections:

**Electrical Section (Rows 25-74)**
• 12V Battery: Single or Dual configuration
• Test Battery: Yes/No - checks battery condition
• Battery Support: Yes/No - keeps battery charged during repair
• Vehicle Type: Gas, Hybrid, or EV
• ADAS: Yes/No - Advanced Driver Assistance Systems

**Vehicle Diagnostics Section (Rows 75-124)**
• Scan Type: Dollar Amount, Labor Unit, or Tesla
• Setup Scan Tool: Yes/No
• Gateway (Unlock): Yes/No - for security gateway vehicles
• ADAS Diagnostics: Yes/No
• Simulate Full Fluids: Yes/No
• Adjust Tire Pressure: Yes/No
• Remove Customer Belongings: Yes/No
• Drive Cycle: Yes/No

**Misc Section (Rows 125-170)**
• Pre Wash: Yes/No
• Bio Hazard: Yes/No
• Shipping/Parts Labels: Yes/No
• Scaffolding: Yes/No

This is usually the first tab you fill out for every estimate.

entries.question: What is the Part Operations tab?
entries.answer:
The **Part Operations** tab is the largest with **161 inputs** for tracking replaced parts.

**Key Concepts:**

**First Panel vs Additional Panel**
• First Panel: Higher labor rate for the first panel in a repair area
• Additional Panel: Lower rate for subsequent panels (overlap credit)
• Example: First fender = 3.0 hrs, additional fender = 2.5 hrs

**Panel Types Covered:**
• Fenders (front/rear)
• Doors (front/rear)
• Quarter panels
• Rocker panels
• Roof panels
• Hood/trunk
• Bumper covers

**Common Inputs:**
• First/Additional Panel selection (A33, A83, A133, A183)
• Yes/No toggles for specific operations
• Multiple rows per panel type for different scenarios

This tab has the most inputs because it handles all panel replacement scenarios.

entries.question: What is the Cover Car tab?
entries.answer:
The **Cover Car** tab has **8 inputs** for masking and protection during paint work.

**Key Inputs:**
• Vehicle Type: Gas or EV (affects masking for high-voltage components)
• Location: Front, Rear, or Full
• Full Cover: Yes/No

**What Cover Car Includes:**
• Masking paper and tape
• Plastic sheeting for windows
• Wheel covers
• Trim protection
• Jamb masking

**When to Use:**
• Any refinish operation
• When painting adjacent panels
• Full repaint jobs
• Whenever overspray is a concern

**Labor Category:** Refinish (P)

entries.question: What is the Body Operations tab?
entries.answer:
The **Body Operations** tab has **25 inputs** for sheet metal and structural work.

**Types of Body Operations:**

**Repair Operations:**
• Dent repair
• Panel straightening
• Sectioning
• Welding

**Structural Operations:**
• Frame rail repair
• Unibody straightening
• Rocker panel sectioning
• Pillar replacement (A, B, C pillars)

**Common Inputs:**
• Yes/No toggles for specific operations
• First/Additional panel selections
• Structural vs cosmetic repair choices

**Labor Category:** Body (B)

**Important:** Structural repairs often require additional scanning and ADAS calibration - make sure SOP List reflects this.

entries.question: What is the Refinish Operations tab?
entries.answer:
The **Refinish Operations** tab has **11 inputs** for paint and finish work.

**Refinish Process Steps:**
1. Surface preparation (sanding, feathering)
2. Primer application
3. Sealer (if needed)
4. Base coat (color)
5. Clear coat
6. Blending (if adjacent panels)
7. Buffing and polishing

**Key Inputs:**
• Paint type: Solid, Metallic, Pearl, Tri-coat
• Blend panels: Yes/No
• Clear coat type: Standard, Matte
• Color match required: Yes/No

**Factors Affecting Time:**
• Panel size
• Number of stages (2-stage, 3-stage)
• Color complexity
• Blending requirements

**Labor Category:** Paint/Refinish (P)

entries.question: What is the Mechanical Operations tab?
entries.answer:
The **Mechanical Operations** tab has **34 inputs** for suspension, steering, and drivetrain work.

**Common Mechanical Operations:**

**Suspension:**
• Strut/shock replacement
• Control arm replacement
• Ball joint replacement
• Wheel bearing replacement
• Subframe R&I or replacement

**Steering:**
• Tie rod replacement
• Steering rack R&I
• Power steering service

**Drivetrain:**
• Axle/CV shaft replacement
• Differential service
• Transmission R&I

**Other:**
• Wheel alignment
• AC evacuation/recharge
• Cooling system service
• Exhaust system work

**Labor Category:** Mechanical (M)

**Note:** Wheel alignment is usually sublet - entered in SOP List under sublet operations.

entries.question: What are the different types of suspension systems?
entries.answer:
**Suspension Types Overview:**

**1. MacPherson Strut** (Most Common)
• Single strut combines shock absorber, coil spring, and steering pivot
• Uses only a lower control arm (no upper arm)
• Found on: Most FWD cars, crossovers, unibody vehicles
• Examples: Honda Civic, Toyota Camry, Ford Escape

**2. Double Wishbone (Double A-Arm)**
• Two control arms - upper and lower
• Separate shock and spring from control arms
• Found on: Trucks with IFS, sports cars, luxury vehicles
• Examples: Toyota Tundra, Honda S2000, Corvette

**3. Solid Axle (Live Axle/Beam Axle)**
• Both wheels connected by rigid beam
• Often paired with leaf springs
• Found on: Truck rear axles, Jeep Wrangler, heavy-duty vehicles
• Examples: Ford F-150 rear, Ram HD front, Jeep Wrangler

**4. Multi-Link**
• 3-5 separate control arms per wheel
• Maximum adjustability and ride quality
• Found on: Rear of luxury cars, sports cars
• Examples: BMW 3-Series rear, Mercedes E-Class rear

**5. Torsion Beam (Twist Beam)**
• H-shaped beam connects trailing arms
• Semi-independent - beam twists to allow some wheel independence
• Found on: Compact FWD cars (rear suspension)
• Examples: VW Golf, Mazda 3, Toyota Corolla

**Collision Repair Note:** Always check suspension after front or side impacts. Strut towers are structural. Never straighten bent suspension components - replace them.

entries.question: What is MacPherson strut suspension?
entries.answer:
**MacPherson Strut Suspension**

Invented by Earle S. MacPherson in 1945, this is the most common front suspension on modern vehicles.

**Key Components:**
• **Strut Assembly** - Vertically oriented tube housing shock absorber, acts as structural link between wheel and body
• **Coil Spring** - Wraps around strut, supports vehicle weight
• **Upper Strut Mount** - Rubber/bearing assembly allowing strut to rotate with steering, bolts to strut tower
• **Lower Control Arm** - A-shaped or L-shaped arm locating bottom of steering knuckle
• **Sway Bar** - Connects left/right suspension to reduce body roll

**How It Works:**
The strut tower mounts directly to the unibody frame as the upper anchor. The lower control arm locates the bottom of the steering knuckle. When you turn the steering wheel, the entire strut assembly pivots on the upper mount.

**Why It's Popular:**
• Compact design - maximizes engine bay and cabin space
• Fewer parts than double wishbone
• Cost effective to manufacture
• Works well with transverse-mounted FWD engines

**Limitations:**
• Limited camber control during cornering
• Strut tower takes up vertical space
• Not usable on body-on-frame vehicles

**Common Vehicles:**
• Honda Civic, Accord (2013+)
• Toyota Camry, RAV4, Corolla
• Ford Escape, Focus
• Porsche 911 (front), BMW M3
• Most FWD cars and crossovers

**Collision Repair Considerations:**
• **Strut tower damage is STRUCTURAL** - requires body labor, not just mechanical
• Check strut tower for buckling after front impacts
• Never straighten a bent strut - always replace
• Strut tower misalignment affects caster (usually non-adjustable)
• Use straight edge along strut cartridge to check for bends
• Measure from hub face to symmetrical points on strut - unequal = bent
• Jounce/rebound camber check: Raise suspension 4", read camber both sides, compress 4", read again. Different readings = bent strut

entries.question: What is double wishbone suspension?
entries.answer:
**Double Wishbone (Double A-Arm) Suspension**

Uses two wishbone-shaped control arms (upper and lower) to connect the wheel to the chassis. Preferred for performance and trucks.

**Key Components:**
• **Upper Control Arm** - A-shaped arm connecting chassis to top of steering knuckle
• **Lower Control Arm** - A-shaped arm connecting chassis to bottom of steering knuckle
• **Ball Joints** - Spherical joints at knuckle end of each control arm (2 per corner)
• **Shock Absorber** - Separate from control arms, mounts between lower arm and chassis/frame
• **Coil Spring** - Can mount on lower arm, upper arm, or around shock
• **Steering Knuckle** - Connects to both upper and lower arms via ball joints

**How It Works:**
Both control arms pivot on bushings at the chassis. The wheel/knuckle is held between them by ball joints. This geometry allows engineers to precisely control camber, caster, and roll center throughout suspension travel.

**Advantages Over MacPherson:**
• Better camber control during cornering (maintains tire contact patch)
• More adjustability for alignment settings
• Lower hood line possible (no strut tower intrusion)
• Preferred for high-performance applications

**Disadvantages:**
• More complex = more expensive
• More components = more potential failure points
• Takes more space than MacPherson
• Heavier than MacPherson

**Common Vehicles:**
• **Trucks (front IFS):** Toyota Tundra, Tacoma, Ford F-150, Chevy Silverado
• **Sports/Luxury:** Corvette (all generations), Audi R8, Ferrari, Aston Martin
• **Honda:** S2000, NSX, older Accord (pre-2013), Acura MDX
• **Body-on-frame SUVs:** Toyota Land Cruiser, 4Runner, Lexus GX

**Collision Repair Considerations:**
• Check BOTH upper and lower control arms - they bend independently
• Inspect all 4 ball joints for damage
• Control arm mounting points on frame/subframe can shift
• More alignment adjustability means more to check
• Place straight edge under lower control arms side-to-side, measure to spring seat - difference indicates bent arm
• Upper arm damage may be hidden - check carefully
• Subframe/engine cradle shift affects both sides equally

entries.question: What is solid axle (live axle) suspension?
entries.answer:
**Solid Axle (Live Axle / Beam Axle) Suspension**

A dependent suspension where both wheels are connected by a single rigid beam or axle housing. When one wheel moves, it affects the other.

**Key Components:**
• **Axle Housing** - Rigid beam containing differential (if driven) or solid tube
• **Leaf Springs** - Flat steel strips stacked together, locate axle and provide spring rate
• **U-Bolts** - Clamp axle to leaf spring pack
• **Shackles** - Allow leaf spring to change length as it flexes
• **Shock Absorbers** - Separate units mounted to axle and frame
• **Track Bar (Panhard Rod)** - Locates axle side-to-side (on coil spring setups)

**How Leaf Springs Work:**
Leaf springs do double duty - they support the vehicle weight AND locate the axle (no need for control arms). The spring mounts to the frame at front with a fixed mount and rear with a shackle that allows the spring to lengthen as it flattens under load.

**Advantages:**
• Extremely strong and durable
• Simple design with fewer parts
• Easy and cheap to repair/modify
• Handles heavy loads well
• Great for towing and hauling
• Maximum wheel travel for off-road

**Disadvantages:**
• Rough ride - when one wheel hits bump, affects other wheel
• Heavy (unsprung weight)
• Differential hangs low (less ground clearance than IFS)
• Less precise handling at highway speeds
• Inter-leaf friction causes harsh low-speed ride

**Common Vehicles:**
• **Rear Axle (most trucks):** Ford F-150, Chevy Silverado, Ram 1500, Toyota Tundra
• **Front Axle:** Ram HD trucks, Jeep Wrangler, Ford Super Duty (older)
• **Both Axles:** Jeep Wrangler, older Land Rover Defender
• **Vans/Commercial:** Most cargo vans, box trucks

**Modern Variations:**
Some trucks use coil springs with solid axle instead of leaf springs (Jeep Wrangler JL, Ram 1500). These need control arms and track bar to locate the axle.

**Collision Repair Considerations:**
• Axle housing/tube can bend from side impacts - check carefully
• Leaf spring hangers (frame mounts) commonly damaged
• Leaf springs can crack or shift - inspect spring eyes and center bolt
• U-bolts may be one-time-use hardware (OEM procedures)
• Check for bent axle by measuring wheel track front vs rear
• Side impacts can push entire axle laterally - check track bar mounts
• Differential cover damage doesn't always mean housing is bent

entries.question: What is multi-link suspension?
entries.answer:
**Multi-Link Suspension**

An independent suspension using 3-5 separate control arms (links) per wheel, allowing precise control of wheel geometry. Most common on rear suspensions of luxury and performance vehicles.

**Key Components:**
• **Upper Lateral Links (2)** - Control upper part of knuckle, locate wheel laterally
• **Lower Lateral Links (2)** - Control lower part of knuckle, carry most of the load
• **Trailing Link** - Locates knuckle fore-aft, absorbs braking torque
• **Toe Link** - Small link specifically controlling toe angle
• **Coil Spring** - Usually mounts on one of the lower links
• **Shock Absorber** - Separate unit between lower link and chassis
• **Subframe** - All links mount to subframe which bolts to body

**How It Works:**
Each link controls a specific aspect of wheel movement. The trailing link handles fore-aft location and braking forces. Lateral links control camber and lateral location. The toe link precisely controls toe angle. Because each link handles specific loads, engineers can tune each aspect independently.

**Why Multi-Link:**
• Each suspension parameter can be tuned independently
• Keeps wheel perpendicular to road during suspension travel
• Maximum tire contact patch through corners
• Best ride quality of any suspension type
• Isolates road noise effectively through multiple bushings

**Disadvantages:**
• Most expensive suspension to manufacture
• Most complex - many parts to inspect/replace
• Heaviest suspension type
• Takes up significant space
• More alignment points = more service time

**Common Vehicles:**
• **Rear:** BMW 3/5/7 Series, Mercedes C/E/S Class, Audi A4/A6
• **Front & Rear:** Tesla Model S/3/X/Y, Porsche Cayenne
• **Rear:** Honda Accord (2018+), Mazda 6, Hyundai Genesis
• **Performance:** Most modern sports cars, supercars

**Collision Repair Considerations:**
• Multiple links = multiple potential damage points
• Each link can be damaged independently
• Subframe mounting points must be checked
• Links have spherical joints (ball joints) or bushings at each end - all need inspection
• Mounting point position tolerances are tight (±1mm)
• Damage to one link often means hidden damage to others
• Complete alignment check required - multiple adjustments per corner
• More labor time for inspection and repair
• Check toe link carefully - small but critical

entries.question: What is torsion beam (twist beam) suspension?
entries.answer:
**Torsion Beam (Twist Beam) Suspension**

A semi-independent rear suspension using an H-shaped or C-shaped beam connecting left and right trailing arms. The beam twists to allow some independence between wheels. Very common on compact FWD vehicles.

**Key Components:**
• **Torsion Beam** - Cross-beam (usually open C-section) that can twist torsionally
• **Trailing Arms** - Arms connecting beam to wheel hubs, pivot at body
• **Coil Springs** - Mount on trailing arms
• **Shock Absorbers** - Mount on trailing arms
• **Pivot Bushings** - Large bushings where trailing arms mount to body

**How It Works:**
The trailing arms pivot at the body and carry the wheel hubs at the rear. The cross-beam connects them, but its cross-section is designed to twist. When one wheel hits a bump, the beam twists rather than forcing the other wheel to move equally. This provides some independence while keeping things simple.

**Built-In Anti-Roll:**
The twisting resistance of the beam acts like a natural sway bar - no separate stabilizer needed.

**Advantages:**
• Simple and inexpensive
• Lightweight
• Compact - doesn't intrude into cargo space
• Fewer parts to wear or fail
• Less under-car clutter
• Proven durability

**Disadvantages:**
• Ride can be harsh (especially low-speed)
• Tends to toe-out during cornering (needs toe-control features)
• Limited tuning options
• Can cause inside wheel lift during hard cornering (hot hatches)
• Not suited for high-powered RWD applications

**Common Vehicles:**
• **Compact Cars:** VW Golf/Jetta (some variants), Mazda 3 (2019+), Toyota Corolla
• **Subcompacts:** Honda Fit, Hyundai Accent, Kia Rio
• **Hot Hatches:** Ford Fiesta ST, VW Polo GTI, Hyundai i20 N, Renault Megane RS
• **Crossovers:** Toyota Corolla Cross, Hyundai Kona

**Collision Repair Considerations:**
• Beam can bend or twist from rear/side impacts
• Check beam for visible deformation - measure side-to-side
• Trailing arm pivot points on body can be damaged
• If beam is bent, entire assembly usually replaced (not repairable)
• Bushings at body pivots commonly damaged in rear impacts
• Rear wheel toe can be affected by beam damage
• Less complex than multi-link but still requires proper inspection
• Check for stress cracks in beam weld areas

entries.question: How do I inspect suspension after a collision?
entries.answer:
**Suspension Inspection After Collision**

Per I-CAR guidelines, wheel alignment should always be checked after any collision that could alter steering or suspension geometry - including tire/wheel impacts, side collisions, or any situation where suspension components are damaged.

**Visual Inspection:**
• Check all control arms for bends, cracks, deformation
• Inspect ball joints and tie rod ends for damage
• Look for leaking struts/shocks (oil on housing)
• Check bushings for tears or displacement
• Inspect mounting points for elongated holes or cracks
• Look at strut towers for buckling or paint cracking
• Compare damaged side to undamaged side

**Measurement Checks:**

**Strut Check:**
• Place straight edge along strut cartridge - gaps indicate bend
• Measure from hub face to symmetrical points on strut - unequal = bent

**Control Arm Check:**
• Place straight edge under both lower control arms (side to side)
• Measure distance up to spring seat on each side
• Difference indicates bent arm

**Jounce/Rebound Camber Check:**
• Raise suspension 4 inches, read camber both sides
• Lower and compress suspension 4 inches, read again
• Different readings side-to-side = bent strut

**Alignment Diagnosis:**
• Steering Axis Inclination (SAI) is usually non-adjustable
• Off-spec SAI can indicate: strut tower misalignment, subframe shift, control arm mount damage
• If camber off on ONE side only = bent spindle, control arm, or strut
• If camber off on BOTH sides = crossmember/subframe shifted

**Critical Rules:**
• **NEVER straighten bent suspension components** - always replace
• Complete ALL structural repairs before alignment
• Document damage with photos and measurements
• Check OEM procedures for one-time-use hardware
• Crash energy travels - check beyond obvious impact area

**What to Document:**
• Photos of all damaged components
• Before/after measurements
• Alignment printout showing out-of-spec readings
• VIN and mileage

**Labor Considerations:**
• Pre-alignment inspection is often separate labor
• Diagnosis time for hidden damage
• Alignment is typically sublet operation

entries.question: What is the SRS Operations tab?
entries.answer:
The **SRS Operations** tab has **9 inputs** for Supplemental Restraint System (airbag) work.

**SRS Components:**
• Driver airbag
• Passenger airbag
• Side curtain airbags
• Knee airbags
• Seat belt pretensioners
• Impact sensors
• SRS module/computer

**Common Operations:**
• Airbag replacement (deployed)
• Seat belt pretensioner replacement
• Clockspring replacement
• SRS module programming
• Impact sensor replacement

**Important Safety Notes:**
• Always disconnect battery first
• Wait 10+ minutes after disconnect
• SRS components are one-time use
• Requires scan tool programming after replacement

**Labor Category:** Mechanical (M)

**Note:** Deployed airbags must be replaced - they cannot be reset.

entries.question: What is the Total Loss Charges tab?
entries.answer:
The **Total Loss Charges** tab has **10 inputs** for vehicles declared a total loss.

**What Total Loss Means:**
• Repair cost exceeds vehicle value (typically 70-80%)
• Vehicle is deemed uneconomical to repair
• Insurance pays Actual Cash Value (ACV)

**Common Total Loss Charges:**
• Administrative fees
• Storage fees
• Towing charges
• Title/registration fees
• Teardown charges (if partially disassembled)
• Parts restocking fees
• Disposal fees

**When to Use:**
• Vehicle is totaled during estimate process
• Supplement reveals total loss condition
• Customer elects to total (owner-retained)

**Note:** Total loss criteria varies by state and insurance company.

entries.question: What is the Body On Frame tab?
entries.answer:
The **Body On Frame** tab has **4 inputs** for trucks and SUVs with separate frames.

**Body On Frame vs Unibody:**
• Body On Frame: Separate frame (trucks, body-on-frame SUVs)
• Unibody: Integrated body/frame (cars, crossovers)

**Common BOF Vehicles:**
• Ford F-150, Super Duty
• Chevrolet Silverado, Tahoe
• Toyota Tundra, 4Runner
• Jeep Wrangler
• Nissan Titan

**BOF-Specific Operations:**
• Frame straightening
• Frame section replacement
• Body mount replacement
• Cab-off repairs
• Box-off repairs

**Key Considerations:**
• Frame damage may require specialized equipment
• Body mounts often need replacement with frame work
• Separate labor for body and frame alignment

**Labor Category:** Body (B) and Mechanical (M)

entries.question: What is the Stolen Recovery tab?
entries.answer:
The **Stolen Recovery** tab has **11 inputs** for theft-related damage.

**Stolen Recovery Scenarios:**

**Partial Strip:**
• Wheels/tires stolen
• Catalytic converter theft
• Interior components missing
• Electronics stolen

**Full Strip:**
• Major components missing (engine, transmission)
• Interior gutted
• Extensive damage from recovery

**Common Operations:**
• Ignition/steering column repair
• Door lock replacement
• Window replacement (entry damage)
• Interior restoration
• Missing parts replacement
• Fingerprint dust cleanup
• Evidence collection hold time

**Special Considerations:**
• Police hold time may apply
• Evidence must be preserved initially
• Photos required for documentation
• Parts verification against VIN

**Note:** Stolen vehicles often have hidden damage - thorough inspection required.

entries.question: What is the 12V Battery input (Single/Dual)?
entries.answer:
The **12V Battery** input (Cell A29) sets the battery configuration:

**Single Battery** (Default)
• Standard for most cars
• Disconnect/Reconnect: 0.4 labor hours
• One 12V battery to disconnect

**Dual Battery**
• Common on: Diesel trucks, heavy-duty vehicles, some hybrids
• Disconnect/Reconnect: 0.8 labor hours
• Two batteries to manage

**When to Select Dual:**
• Ford Super Duty diesels
• RAM 2500/3500 diesels
• GM Duramax trucks
• Some Mercedes diesels
• Vehicles with auxiliary battery

**Why Battery Disconnect Matters:**
• Prevents electrical shorts during welding
• Protects modules during repairs
• Required before airbag work
• Safety requirement for most collision repairs

entries.question: What is the Vehicle Type input (Gas/Hybrid/EV)?
entries.answer:
The **Vehicle Type** input (Cell A35) sets the powertrain type:

**Gas** (Default)
• Standard internal combustion engine
• Normal battery disconnect procedures
• Standard repair procedures

**Hybrid**
• Has both gas engine AND high-voltage battery
• Requires HV system disable procedures
• Orange cables = high voltage!
• Special PPE may be required
• Additional labor for HV safety

**EV (Electric Vehicle)**
• 100% electric - high-voltage only
• Must disable HV system before ANY repair
• Requires certified EV technician
• Special equipment needed
• Significantly more labor for safety procedures

**Important:**
• NEVER touch orange cables without training
• HV batteries can be lethal
• EVs may require OEM-specific procedures
• Some insurers require OEM-certified shops for EV repairs

entries.question: What is the ADAS input?
entries.answer:
The **ADAS** input (Cell C29) indicates if ADAS calibration is needed:

**What is ADAS?**
Advanced Driver Assistance Systems - safety features using cameras and sensors:
• Forward collision warning
• Automatic emergency braking
• Lane departure warning
• Lane keep assist
• Adaptive cruise control
• Blind spot monitoring
• Parking sensors
• 360° camera systems

**When ADAS Calibration is Required:**
• Windshield replacement
• Front bumper work (radar)
• Mirror replacement (cameras)
• Wheel alignment
• Any structural repair
• Suspension work

**Types of Calibration:**
• **Static**: Done in shop with targets, controlled environment
• **Dynamic**: Requires test drive at specific speeds

**Labor:** Typically 0.5 - 2.0 hours depending on system

**Select YES if** the vehicle has ANY of these features.

entries.question: What is the Scan Type input?
entries.answer:
The **Scan Type** input (Cell A79) determines how diagnostic scans are priced:

**Dollar Amount** (Default)
• Fixed price for scans
• Example: $50 pre-scan, $50 post-scan
• Common with third-party scan tools
• Easier to estimate

**Labor Unit**
• Billed as labor hours
• Example: 0.5 hrs pre-scan, 0.5 hrs post-scan
• Uses shop's labor rate
• May be preferred by some insurers

**Tesla**
• Special pricing for Tesla vehicles
• Tesla requires their diagnostic system
• May require Tesla Mobile Service
• Often higher cost

**Pre-Scan vs Post-Scan:**
• **Pre-Scan**: Documents existing codes BEFORE repair
• **Post-Scan**: Clears codes and verifies systems AFTER repair
• Both are required by most OEMs and insurers

entries.question: What is Gateway (Unlock)?
entries.answer:
The **Gateway (Unlock)** input (Cell A87) is for security gateway vehicles:

**What is a Security Gateway?**
A firewall that blocks third-party scan tools from accessing vehicle modules.

**Affected Manufacturers:**
• FCA/Stellantis (Chrysler, Dodge, Jeep, RAM) - 2018+
• Some GM vehicles - 2020+
• Some Ford vehicles - 2021+
• Expanding to more brands

**Why It Exists:**
• Prevents unauthorized module programming
• Protects against theft/hacking
• Cybersecurity requirement

**How to Unlock:**
• AutoAuth subscription (paid service)
• OEM scan tool (expensive)
• Dealer service (time-consuming)

**Select YES if:**
• FCA/Stellantis 2018 or newer
• Vehicle requires module programming
• Third-party scan tool shows "gateway blocked"

**Labor:** Additional time for unlock procedure

entries.question: What is First Panel vs Additional Panel?
entries.answer:
**First Panel vs Additional Panel** (Part Operations tab) affects labor rates:

**First Panel**
• Full labor time for the first panel replaced
• Includes all setup and prep time
• Higher rate applies
• Example: Replace first door = 4.0 hours

**Additional Panel**
• Reduced labor for subsequent panels
• Overlap credit - some work already done
• Lower rate applies
• Example: Replace second door = 3.2 hours

**Why the Difference?**
When replacing multiple adjacent panels:
• Setup only done once
• Adjacent panel removal already partially done
• Some prep work overlaps
• Efficiency gains on subsequent panels

**Examples:**
• First fender + second fender: First gets full rate
• Door + adjacent quarter: First panel rate on one
• Front bumper + fender: May share R&I operations

**Important:** Always select **First Panel** for the first panel in an area, then **Additional Panel** for the rest.

entries.question: What are the different operation types?
entries.answer:
There are several operation types used in collision repair estimating:

**Rpr (Repair)** - Fix existing damaged parts without replacing
• Example: Repair dent in fender
• Typically less expensive than replacement
• Preserves original parts

**Rpl (Replace)** - Remove damaged part and install new one
• Example: Replace bumper cover
• Includes R&I (Remove & Install) time
• New part cost added

**Rfn (Refinish)** - Paint and finish work
• Example: Blend adjacent panel
• Measured in refinish hours
• Separate labor category (P)

**R&I (Remove & Install)** - Remove part to access damage, reinstall same part
• Example: R&I headlight to access fender
• Part is reused, not replaced
• Common for access operations

**Sublet** - Work sent to outside vendor
• Example: Wheel alignment, AC recharge, glass
• Billed at actual cost plus markup
• Listed separately on estimate

entries.question: What are the labor categories (M, B, P)?
entries.answer:
Labor is categorized into three main types:

**M - Mechanical Labor**
• Suspension, steering, drivetrain work
• Battery disconnect/reconnect
• ADAS calibration
• AC service
• Wheel alignment
• Engine/transmission work
• Typical rate: $75-125/hour

**B - Body Labor**
• Sheet metal repair and replacement
• Structural work (frame, unibody)
• Panel alignment
• Welding
• Dent repair
• Typical rate: $50-75/hour

**P - Paint/Refinish Labor**
• Painting and clear coat
• Blending
• Color matching
• Surface prep
• Masking (cover car)
• Typical rate: $50-75/hour

**Why Categories Matter:**
• Each shop has different hourly rates per category
• Insurers negotiate rates by category
• Total labor = M hours × M rate + B hours × B rate + P hours × P rate

entries.question: What are the Misc operations in SOP List?
entries.answer:
The **Misc section** (Rows 125-170) includes additional operations:

**Pre Wash**
• Wash vehicle before repair begins
• Removes dirt, debris, contaminants
• Helps identify hidden damage
• Labor: ~0.5 hours

**Bio Hazard**
• Blood, bodily fluids in vehicle
• Requires special cleaning/disposal
• PPE required for technicians
• Additional labor and materials

**Shipping/Parts Labels**
• Label parts for tracking
• Important for complex repairs
• Helps with parts returns
• Organization during disassembly

**Scaffolding**
• For large vehicles (box trucks, RVs)
• Roof access on tall vehicles
• Safety equipment for height work
• Additional rental/setup cost

**These are often overlooked** - make sure to include when applicable!

entries.question: What are the Vehicle Diagnostics operations?
entries.answer:
The **Vehicle Diagnostics** section (Rows 75-124) includes:

**Setup Scan Tool** (A81)
• Configure scan tool for vehicle
• Connect and initialize
• Labor: ~0.2 hours

**ADAS Diagnostics** (B79)
• Scan ADAS-specific systems
• Check camera/sensor calibration status
• Verify ADAS codes

**Simulate Full Fluids** (B81)
• Top off fluids for accurate diagnostics
• Required for some system tests
• Prevents false codes

**Adjust Tire Pressure** (B83)
• Set to spec for diagnostics
• Required for TPMS reset
• Affects ADAS calibration

**Remove Customer Belongings** (B85)
• Clear interior for repair access
• Document/inventory items
• Labor for large amounts

**Drive Cycle** (B87)
• Test drive after repairs
• Required for some calibrations
• Verifies systems function
• Dynamic ADAS calibration

entries.question: How do the outputs/calculations work?
entries.answer:
Each tab calculates **output values** automatically based on your inputs:

**Summary Row (typically Row 27)**
Displays totals for the current tab:
• Total Operations count
• Total Price ($)
• Total Labor hours
• Total Refinish hours

**Per-Operation Outputs (Columns M-X)**
• Column M: Operation type (Rpr, Rpl, R&I)
• Column O: Description
• Column Q: Quantity
• Column R: Price
• Column V: Labor hours
• Column W: Labor category (M, B, P)
• Column X: Refinish hours

**How Formulas Work:**
• IF statements check your inputs
• Example: IF(A29="Dual", 0.8, 0.4)
• Selecting "Dual" battery = 0.8 labor hours
• Selecting "Single" = 0.4 labor hours

**Operations appear only when** the corresponding input is filled out. Empty inputs = no operation generated.

entries.question: How do I export to CCC or Mitchell?
entries.answer:
The **McStud Tool** exports operations to CCC or Mitchell:

**Workflow:**
1. Fill out inputs in Excel (or McStud pages)
2. Select operations in the output area
3. Copy to clipboard (Ctrl+C)
4. Click CCC Desktop, CCC Web, or Mitchell button
5. Operations auto-type into target system

**Export Buttons:**
• **CCC Desktop** - For CCC ONE desktop application
• **CCC Web** - For CCC web browser version
• **Mitchell** - For Mitchell estimating system

**Speed Settings:**
• Slow - Most reliable, for older systems
• Normal - Default balance
• Fast - For modern systems
• Turbo - Maximum speed

**Tips:**
• Click into CCC/Mitchell FIRST
• Don't touch keyboard during export
• Use Preview to verify before export
• Check Statistics to track accuracy

entries.question: Tips for accurate estimates?
entries.answer:
**Best Practices for Accurate Estimates:**

**Start with SOP List:**
• Always fill out SOP List first
• Battery disconnect on almost every job
• Pre/Post scans are standard now
• ADAS if vehicle has any safety features

**Don't Miss These:**
• Gateway unlock (FCA 2018+)
• Drive cycle for calibrations
• Tire pressure for ADAS
• EV/Hybrid safety procedures
• Bio hazard when applicable

**Part Operations:**
• First panel gets full rate
• Additional panels get overlap rate
• Check ALL related operations

**Verify Outputs:**
• Review generated operations
• Check labor category assignments
• Confirm prices match current rates

**Statistics Feature:**
• Compare IN (imported) vs OUT (exported)
• Should match if everything transferred
• Discrepancies indicate missed items

**Common Mistakes:**
• Forgetting ADAS calibration
• Missing battery disconnect
• Wrong panel tier selection
• Not including pre/post scans

entries.question: What are sublet operations?
entries.answer:
Sublet operations are work sent to outside specialists:

**Common Sublet Items:**
• Wheel alignment
• AC evacuation and recharge
• Glass replacement (windshield, windows)
• PDR (Paintless Dent Repair)
• Upholstery/interior repair
• Suspension geometry
• Key programming
• ADAS calibration (some shops)

**How Sublet is Billed:**
• Actual invoice cost from vendor
• Shop may add markup (typically 10-25%)
• Listed separately on estimate
• Requires documentation/invoice

**Why Sublet?**
• Specialized equipment needed
• Expertise not in-house
• More cost-effective
• Faster turnaround
• Liability reasons (glass, alignment)

**In SOP List:**
Sublet items may have their own inputs or be handled in the pricing/notes section.

entries.question: What does blend mean in refinish?
entries.answer:
**Blending** is a paint technique for seamless color matching:

**Why Blend?**
• New paint may not match aged paint exactly
• Sun fading affects color over time
• Different paint batches can vary slightly
• Metallic/pearl colors show mismatch easily

**How Blending Works:**
1. Adjacent panel is prepped (scuffed)
2. Color is sprayed lighter toward edges
3. Clear coat extends further than color
4. Creates invisible transition

**When to Blend:**
• Replacing a panel next to original paint
• Metallic/pearl colors (more visible mismatch)
• High-visibility areas (hood, doors)
• Customer requests perfect match

**Blend Allowance:**
• Typically 0.5-1.5 hours per panel
• Adjacent panels only
• May blend 2-3 panels for best match

**Refinish Operations Tab:**
Use this to set blend requirements for the estimate.

entries.question: How do the Statistics work?
entries.answer:
The **Statistics** feature tracks your import/export activity:

**What it Shows:**
• **IN (Import)**: Operations copied from Excel
• **OUT (Export)**: Operations sent to CCC/Mitchell

**Columns Tracked:**
• Operations count
• Total Price
• Labor hours
• Paint/Refinish hours

**Match Indicator:**
• Green ✓ = All values match (accurate!)
• Yellow ⚠ = Differences detected (check for missed items)

**Time Periods:**
• Today
• This Week
• This Month
• This Year
• All Time

**Target Filter:**
• All
• CCC Desktop
• CCC Web
• Mitchell

**Use Statistics to:**
• Verify exports completed correctly
• Track daily productivity
• Identify if operations were missed

entries.question: What is Estimate Upload?
entries.answer:
The **Estimate Upload** feature allows you to import existing estimates:

**Purpose:**
• Import estimates from files
• Load previous work
• Review existing estimates

**Supported Formats:**
• May vary by version
• Check the upload panel for accepted file types

**How to Use:**
1. Expand the Estimate Upload section
2. Select your file
3. Review imported data
4. Make any needed adjustments

**Note:** This feature is marked as NEW and may have additional capabilities added over time.

entries.question: Where do I find bumper operations?
entries.answer:
**Bumper Operations** are found in the **Part Operations** tab:

**Location:** Part Operations tab, look for Bumper sections

**Common Bumper Operations:**
• **Bumper Cover R&I** - Remove and reinstall (access for other repairs)
• **Bumper Cover Replace** - Remove damaged, install new
• **Bumper Reinforcement** - The structural bar behind the cover
• **Energy Absorber** - Foam/honeycomb between cover and reinforcement

**Associated Operations:**
• Fog lights R&I
• Parking sensors (if equipped)
• Grille R&I or Replace
• License plate bracket
• Tow hook cover

**What to Remember:**
• Front bumpers often have ADAS sensors (radar)
• Rear bumpers may have parking sensors
• Check for sensor calibration needs
• Reinforcement damage = possible frame check

**Labor Category:** Body (B) for R&I and Replace

**First Panel vs Additional:**
Bumper cover is typically counted separately from fenders/panels for overlap purposes.

entries.question: What are OEM procedures?
entries.answer:
**OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) Procedures** are repair instructions from the vehicle manufacturer:

**What They Include:**
• Step-by-step repair instructions
• Required tools and equipment
• Torque specifications
• Weld locations and types
• Safety warnings
• Part replacement requirements

**Why They Matter:**
• Legal liability protection
• Warranty compliance
• Proper repair quality
• Insurance requirements
• Safety assurance

**Where to Find Them:**
• OEM repair websites (fee-based)
• I-CAR RTS portal
• Collision repair networks
• Dealer technical resources

**Common OEM Sites:**
• Honda: techinfo.honda.com
• Toyota: techinfo.toyota.com
• Ford: motorcraftservice.com
• GM: acdelcotds.com

**Always check OEM procedures** before structural repairs, welding, or safety system work!

entries.question: What is a supplement?
entries.answer:
A **Supplement** is an addition to the original estimate after hidden damage is found:

**When Supplements Happen:**
• During teardown/disassembly
• After removing damaged parts
• When accessing inner structure
• Finding rust or prior damage
• Parts don't fit as expected

**Common Supplement Items:**
• Inner structure damage
• Wiring harness damage
• Suspension damage behind panels
• Corrosion protection needs
• Additional blend panels
• Calibration requirements

**Supplement Process:**
1. Document new damage (photos!)
2. Add operations to estimate
3. Contact insurance adjuster
4. Get approval before proceeding
5. Update repair plan

**Tips:**
• Always photograph before AND after disassembly
• Document part numbers
• Note discovery date/time
• Keep damaged parts for inspection

entries.question: How is labor time determined?
entries.answer:
**Labor Time** in collision repair comes from several sources:

**Published Times (Database):**
• CCC, Mitchell, Audatex publish standard times
• Based on average skilled technician
• Assumes clean, undamaged vehicle
• Industry-accepted benchmarks

**Judgment Time:**
• For operations not in database
• Estimator determines based on experience
• May require negotiation with insurer
• Document reasoning

**Factors That Affect Time:**
• Vehicle complexity
• Access difficulty
• Rust or corrosion
• Previous repairs
• Aftermarket parts fitment

**Time Included vs Not Included:**
• Included: Standard R&I, basic prep
• NOT Included: Corrosion protection, seam sealer, sound deadener, scan/calibration

**Overlap:**
• When operations share steps
• Adjustments prevent double-billing
• Example: Adjacent panel removal

entries.question: What is corrosion protection?
entries.answer:
**Corrosion Protection** prevents rust on repaired areas:

**Types of Protection:**

**Weld-Through Primer**
• Applied before welding
• Protects weld joints
• Zinc-based formula

**Cavity Wax**
• Sprayed inside closed sections
• Pillars, rocker panels, rails
• Prevents internal rust

**Seam Sealer**
• Covers welded seams
• Matches OEM appearance
• Prevents water intrusion

**E-Coat / Primer**
• Factory-style protection
• Applied to bare metal
• Base for paint system

**When Required:**
• Any bare metal exposure
• Welded areas
• Panel replacement
• Structural repairs

**NOT Included in labor times** - must be added separately!

**Labor Category:** Body (B) or Refinish (P) depending on operation

entries.question: What's the difference between R&I and Replace?
entries.answer:
**R&I (Remove & Install)** vs **Replace** - key differences:

**R&I (Remove & Install)**
• Remove part to access damage
• Reinstall the SAME part
• Part is reused
• No part cost
• Used for access operations
• Example: R&I headlight to repair fender

**Replace (Rpl)**
• Remove damaged part
• Install NEW part
• Part cost added
• Includes R&I labor
• Used when part is damaged
• Example: Replace cracked bumper cover

**When to Use Each:**

**Use R&I when:**
• Part is not damaged
• Need access to repair area
• Part can be safely removed/reinstalled

**Use Replace when:**
• Part is damaged beyond repair
• Part is one-time-use (clips, fasteners)
• Cost to repair exceeds replacement
• Safety component (airbags, sensors)

**R&I is included in Replace** - don't add both!

entries.question: What's included vs not included in labor?
entries.answer:
Understanding what's **Included** vs **Not Included** in published labor times:

**INCLUDED in Labor:**
• Basic R&I of adjacent parts
• Standard preparation
• Installing new part
• Basic alignment/adjustment
• Standard fasteners
• Clean up of work area

**NOT INCLUDED (Add Separately):**
• Corrosion protection
• Seam sealer application
• Cavity wax
• Sound deadener
• Undercoating
• Pin striping/decals
• Protective film
• Pre/Post scanning
• ADAS calibration
• Wheel alignment
• Hazardous material handling
• Structural measuring

**Check P-Pages:**
• CCC, Mitchell, Audatex have "P-Pages"
• Lists what IS and ISN'T included
• Varies by estimating system
• Reference when negotiating

**Tip:** Always review P-Pages for each operation to ensure nothing is missed!

entries.question: What is an estimate?
entries.answer:
An **Estimate** is a detailed breakdown of repair costs:

**Components of an Estimate:**
• Labor operations (hours × rate)
• Parts (cost + markup)
• Materials (paint, supplies)
• Sublet operations
• Taxes (where applicable)

**Types of Estimates:**

**Preliminary/Initial**
• Based on visible damage
• Before disassembly
• May change with supplements

**Revised/Supplemental**
• Updated after hidden damage found
• Additional operations added
• Requires approval

**Final**
• All damage accounted for
• Used for billing
• Matches completed repairs

**Estimate vs Invoice:**
• Estimate = projected cost
• Invoice = actual charges
• Should closely match

**Who Uses Estimates:**
• Insurance adjusters
• Shop management
• Customers
• Parts departments

entries.question: How are paint materials calculated?
entries.answer:
**Paint Materials** are calculated separately from labor:

**Calculation Methods:**

**Refinish Hour Based**
• Materials = Refinish Hours × Material Rate
• Example: 5.0 Rfn hrs × $40/hr = $200 materials
• Most common method

**Per Panel**
• Flat rate per panel painted
• Varies by panel size
• Example: $75/small, $125/large

**Actual Cost**
• Based on paint mixed
• Requires documentation
• Used for custom colors

**What Materials Include:**
• Primer/sealer
• Base coat (color)
• Clear coat
• Reducer/hardener
• Masking materials
• Sandpaper/supplies
• Tack cloths
• Mixing cups

**Material Rate:**
• Negotiated with insurers
• Varies by region
• Typically $28-50/refinish hour

**Tip:** Materials are often underpaid - track actual costs to negotiate better rates!

entries.question: What is betterment/depreciation?
entries.answer:
**Betterment** (also called depreciation) is a deduction for improved condition:

**What It Means:**
• Insurance pays to restore to PRE-LOSS condition
• If new part improves vehicle, owner pays difference
• Controversial practice
• Varies by state/insurer

**Common Betterment Items:**
• Tires (based on tread wear)
• Battery (based on age)
• Brake components
• Suspension parts
• Exhaust systems

**Example:**
• 5-year-old tire needs replacement
• New tire costs $200
• Insurance may pay only $100 (50% worn)
• Customer pays $100 betterment

**When It Applies:**
• Wear items only
• Usually older vehicles
• Based on documented condition

**Disputes:**
• Some states limit betterment
• Document pre-loss condition
• Check state insurance regulations
• Customer can dispute with insurer

entries.question: When is a vehicle considered a total loss?
entries.answer:
A vehicle is **Totaled** when repair cost exceeds a threshold:

**Total Loss Threshold:**
• Varies by state: 50% to 100% of ACV
• Most common: 70-80%
• Some use Total Loss Formula (TLF)

**Total Loss Formula:**
Cost of Repair + Salvage Value > Actual Cash Value

**Example:**
• Vehicle ACV: $20,000
• Repair estimate: $14,000 (70%)
• Salvage value: $4,000
• $14,000 + $4,000 = $18,000 > threshold
• = Total Loss

**Factors Considered:**
• Actual Cash Value (ACV)
• Repair costs (including supplements)
• Salvage value
• State regulations
• Insurer guidelines

**ACV Determination:**
• Comparable vehicle sales
• Mileage adjustment
• Condition adjustment
• Options/features

**Customer Options:**
• Accept total loss payout
• Owner-retained salvage
• Dispute ACV valuation

entries.question: What is a DRP (Direct Repair Program)?
entries.answer:
**DRP (Direct Repair Program)** is a partnership between shops and insurers:

**How It Works:**
• Shop agrees to insurer's terms
• Insurer refers customers to shop
• Streamlined claim process
• Performance metrics tracked

**Benefits for Shop:**
• Steady work referrals
• Faster payment
• Direct communication with insurer
• Photo-based approvals

**Requirements:**
• Meet quality standards
• Achieve cycle time targets
• Customer satisfaction scores
• Compliance with guidelines
• Often: discounted rates

**Potential Downsides:**
• Lower labor rates
• Pressure on cycle time
• Insurer influence on repairs
• Parts source requirements

**Customer Choice:**
• Customers can choose ANY shop
• Not required to use DRP shop
• DRP is convenience, not requirement

**Non-DRP Shops:**
• Can repair any vehicle
• May have longer approval process
• Full rate negotiations

entries.question: What is cycle time?
entries.answer:
**Cycle Time** measures how long repairs take:

**Types of Cycle Time:**

**Keys-to-Keys**
• From vehicle drop-off to pick-up
• Includes all delays
• Customer's perspective

**Touch Time**
• Actual hands-on repair time
• Excludes waiting/delays
• Shop efficiency measure

**Work Days**
• Business days in shop
• Excludes weekends
• Common metric

**Factors Affecting Cycle Time:**
• Parts availability
• Supplements/approvals
• Shop workload
• Repair complexity
• Sublet operations
• Weather (paint booth)

**Industry Benchmarks:**
• Light damage: 3-5 days
• Medium damage: 7-10 days
• Heavy damage: 14-21+ days

**Why It Matters:**
• Customer satisfaction
• Rental car costs
• Insurance metrics
• Shop throughput
• DRP performance

entries.question: What are the different parts types?
entries.answer:
**Parts Types** used in collision repair:

**OEM (Original Equipment)**
• Made by vehicle manufacturer
• Exact fit guaranteed
• Highest cost
• Full warranty
• Required for some repairs

**Aftermarket (A/M)**
• Made by third parties
• Lower cost (20-50% less)
• Fit may vary
• CAPA/NSF certified = better quality
• Common on older vehicles

**Recycled/Used (LKQ)**
• From salvage vehicles
• Significant savings (50-75%)
• Quality varies by source
• Includes prep labor
• Good for older vehicles

**Remanufactured (Reman)**
• Rebuilt to spec
• Common for mechanical parts
• Warranty included
• Headlights, wheels, etc.

**OPT OEM**
• OEM at discounted price
• Competitive with aftermarket
• Same quality as OEM

**Customer Rights:**
• Can request OEM parts
• May pay difference in cost
• Check policy language

entries.question: What is parts markup?
entries.answer:
**Parts Markup** is the profit added to part cost:

**How It Works:**
• Shop buys part at cost
• Sells to customer at higher price
• Difference = markup/profit

**Typical Markups:**
• OEM parts: 25-40%
• Aftermarket: 30-50%
• Recycled/Used: 25-35%
• Sublet: 10-25%

**Calculation:**
• Cost: $100
• Markup: 30%
• Sell price: $130
• Profit: $30

**List Price vs Net:**
• List price = MSRP/retail
• Net price = shop's cost
• Markup based on net cost

**Insurer Guidelines:**
• Many cap markup at 25-30%
• Negotiable
• Part of DRP agreements

**Why Markup Matters:**
• Major shop revenue source
• Covers handling, storage
• Offsets labor rate limits
• Business sustainability

entries.question: What is a three-way match?
entries.answer:
**Three-Way Match** is a parts verification process:

**The Three Ways:**
1. **Part Number** - Matches order
2. **Vehicle** - Fits this specific car
3. **Estimate** - Matches what was written

**Why It Matters:**
• Prevents wrong parts installed
• Catches ordering errors early
• Avoids supplement delays
• Reduces comebacks

**When to Verify:**
• When parts arrive
• Before repair starts
• After teardown (new damage?)

**What to Check:**
• Part number on box vs invoice
• Part vs old part comparison
• Color code (if applicable)
• Left vs right side
• Model year compatibility

**Mirror Match:**
• Compare new part to old
• Check mounting points
• Verify connectors/clips
• Same size/shape

**Document Issues:**
• Photo mismatched parts
• Note on estimate
• Contact vendor immediately

entries.question: What is blueprinting?
entries.answer:
**Blueprinting** is complete damage analysis through teardown:

**Purpose:**
• Identify ALL damage upfront
• Reduce supplements
• Order all parts at once
• Accurate repair planning
• Faster cycle time

**Process:**
1. Initial estimate/photos
2. Full disassembly
3. Document all damage
4. Write complete estimate
5. Order all parts
6. Begin repairs when parts arrive

**Benefits:**
• One supplement (or none)
• Parts ready when needed
• Accurate labor planning
• Better customer communication
• Reduced cycle time

**What to Document:**
• Hidden structural damage
• Wiring/connector damage
• Corrosion issues
• Prior repairs
• All affected systems

**100% Blueprint:**
• Every panel removed
• All damage found
• Complete parts list
• Full labor assessment

entries.question: What is quality control in collision repair?
entries.answer:
**Quality Control (QC)** ensures repairs meet standards:

**QC Checkpoints:**

**Pre-Repair:**
• Damage documented
• Parts verified
• OEM procedures reviewed

**During Repair:**
• Welds inspected
• Measurements verified
• Fit checked before paint

**Pre-Paint:**
• Body work smooth
• Gaps/alignment correct
• Surface prep complete

**Post-Paint:**
• Color match verified
• No defects (orange peel, runs)
• Blend acceptable

**Final Inspection:**
• All operations complete
• Fasteners torqued
• Trim/moldings installed
• Systems functional
• Clean inside and out
• Post-scan clear
• Test drive complete

**Documentation:**
• QC checklist signed
• Photos at each stage
• Scan reports saved
• Calibration records

entries.question: What are common insurance terms?
entries.answer:
**Common Insurance Terms** in collision repair:

**Coverage Types:**
• **Collision**: Damage from impact
• **Comprehensive**: Non-collision (theft, hail, vandalism)
• **Liability**: Other party's damage you caused

**Parties:**
• **Insured**: Policy holder
• **Claimant**: Person filing claim
• **First Party**: Your own insurance
• **Third Party**: Other driver's insurance

**Key Terms:**
• **Deductible**: Amount customer pays first
• **ACV**: Actual Cash Value (vehicle worth)
• **Subrogation**: Insurer recovers from at-fault party
• **Adjuster**: Insurance rep who handles claims
• **Appraiser**: Writes damage estimate

**Claim Process:**
1. Report accident
2. Claim number assigned
3. Vehicle inspected
4. Estimate written
5. Repairs authorized
6. Supplements approved
7. Final payment issued

**Deductible:**
• Typically $250-$1,000
• Paid by customer
• May be waived if not at fault

entries.question: What are common estimating mistakes?
entries.answer:
**Common Estimating Mistakes** to avoid:

**Often Forgotten:**
• Battery disconnect/reconnect
• Pre/Post scan
• ADAS calibration
• Corrosion protection
• Seam sealer
• Sound deadener
• Wheel alignment
• Drive cycle

**Wrong Selections:**
• First Panel when should be Additional
• Single battery on diesel truck
• Gas when vehicle is Hybrid/EV
• Missing Gateway for FCA 2018+

**Documentation:**
• Not enough photos
• Missing measurements
• No OEM procedure reference
• Incomplete supplements

**Labor Issues:**
• Overlap not accounted for
• Included operations added separately
• Wrong labor category
• Missing judgment time

**Parts:**
• Wrong part number
• Missing hardware/clips
• Not checking part condition

**Prevention:**
• Use checklists (McStud SOP List!)
• Review before submitting
• Check P-Pages
• Follow OEM procedures

entries.question: Tips for negotiating with insurers?
entries.answer:
**Negotiation Tips** when insurers push back:

**Document Everything:**
• Photos, photos, photos
• OEM procedure printouts
• Position statements
• Written communication

**Know Your Sources:**
• P-Pages (what's included/not)
• OEM repair procedures
• I-CAR guidelines
• DEG (Database Enhancement Gateway) inquiries

**Be Professional:**
• Facts, not emotions
• Written requests
• Specific references
• Follow up consistently

**Common Disputes:**
• Scan fees → OEM position statements
• Calibration → OEM requirements
• Corrosion protection → I-CAR standards
• Labor time → Judgment time documentation

**Escalation Path:**
1. Field adjuster
2. Supervisor
3. Regional manager
4. State insurance commissioner
5. Legal action (last resort)

**Customer Involvement:**
• Customer can dispute directly
• It's THEIR policy
• Shop advocates, customer decides

entries.question: Why is a pre-repair scan important?
entries.answer:
**Pre-Repair Scan** documents vehicle condition BEFORE repairs:

**Why It's Critical:**
• Documents pre-existing codes
• Identifies collision-related codes
• Required by most OEMs
• Establishes baseline
• Protects shop from liability

**What It Captures:**
• Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs)
• Module communication status
• System functionality
• Calibration status

**OEM Requirements:**
• Nearly ALL manufacturers require it
• Position statements available
• Part of proper repair procedure

**Documentation:**
• Save scan report (PDF)
• Include in repair file
• Note date and time
• List all modules scanned

**If Insurer Denies:**
• Reference OEM position statement
• Provide I-CAR documentation
• Note liability concerns
• Customer safety issue

**Typical Labor:**
• 0.3-0.5 hours
• Or $50-100 flat rate
• Depends on vehicle/scan tool

entries.question: Why is a post-repair scan important?
entries.answer:
**Post-Repair Scan** verifies systems after repairs:

**Purpose:**
• Clear repair-related codes
• Verify all systems functional
• Confirm calibrations complete
• Document vehicle ready for delivery

**What to Check:**
• All DTCs cleared
• No new codes present
• Modules communicating
• SRS system ready
• ADAS systems operational

**Required Before Delivery:**
• Safety system verification
• Warranty compliance
• Liability protection
• Quality assurance

**Documentation:**
• Clean scan report
• Save to file
• Customer sign-off
• Keep for records

**If Codes Present:**
• Investigate cause
• May need additional repairs
• Don't deliver with active codes
• Especially SRS/ADAS codes

**Combined Pre + Post:**
• Shows before/after comparison
• Proves repair effectiveness
• Professional documentation

entries.question: Where do I find welding operations?
entries.answer:
**Welding operations** are in the **Body Operations** tab.

**Location:** Body Operations tab (Tab 4)

**Welding Types in Collision Repair:**

**MIG (GMAW)**
• Most common in shops
• Gas Metal Arc Welding
• Continuous wire feed

**Squeeze-Type Resistance (STR)**
• Factory-style spot welds
• Required by many OEMs

**Plug Weld**
• Hole drilled in top panel
• Welded to panel below

**Where You'll Need Welding:**
• Panel replacement
• Structural repairs
• Quarter panel sectioning
• Rocker replacement
• Pillar repairs

**Always check OEM procedures** for weld specifications!

**See Also:** Body Operations tab for welding-related inputs.

entries.question: What are the different weld types?
entries.answer:
**Weld Types** in collision repair:

**MIG (GMAW)**
• Most common in shops
• Gas Metal Arc Welding
• Continuous wire feed
• Strong, versatile

**Squeeze-Type Resistance (STR)**
• Factory-style spot welds
• Required by many OEMs
• Special equipment needed
• Replicates original welds

**Plug Weld**
• Hole drilled in top panel
• Welded to panel below
• Common replacement for spot welds
• When STR not available

**Spot Weld**
• Original factory weld
• Removed with cutter/drill
• Replaced with STR or plug weld

**Silicon Bronze**
• Low heat application
• Used on aluminum
• Prevents distortion

**OEM Requirements:**
• Specify weld type and location
• Number of welds required
• Weld spacing
• Heat restrictions (especially aluminum)

**Always check OEM procedures** for weld specifications!

entries.question: What's special about aluminum repair?
entries.answer:
**Aluminum Repair** requires special procedures:

**Key Differences from Steel:**
• Different welding techniques
• Dedicated tools required
• No cross-contamination
• Special rivets and adhesives
• More heat-sensitive

**Dedicated Area Required:**
• Separate from steel work
• Aluminum-only tools
• Clean environment
• Prevents galvanic corrosion

**Common Aluminum Vehicles:**
• Ford F-150 (2015+)
• Audi (many models)
• Tesla (all models)
• Jaguar/Land Rover
• Luxury vehicles

**Repair Methods:**
• Rivet bonding
• MIG welding (special wire)
• Adhesive joining
• Self-piercing rivets
• Flow-form rivets

**Certifications Needed:**
• OEM-specific training
• Aluminum repair certification
• Equipment requirements

**If Not Certified:**
• May need to sublet
• OEM network shop
• Specialty facility

entries.question: What is the Test Battery operation?
entries.answer:
**Test Battery** (Cell A31) checks battery condition:

**What It Does:**
• Tests battery health
• Checks charge level
• Measures cranking amps
• Identifies weak batteries

**When to Test:**
• Vehicle was sitting (storage)
• Accessories left on during repair
• Older battery
• Electrical system repairs
• Customer complaint

**Why It Matters:**
• Weak battery = diagnostic issues
• False codes possible
• May not start after repair
• Customer satisfaction

**Test Results:**
• Good: Battery OK
• Weak: May need replacement
• Bad: Replace before delivery
• Recharge: Needs charging

**Labor:** Typically 0.2-0.3 hours

**Select YES when:**
• Vehicle older than 3-4 years
• Extended repair time
• Any electrical symptoms
• Battery was disconnected long-term

entries.question: What is Battery Support?
entries.answer:
**Battery Support** (Cell A33) keeps battery charged during repair:

**What It Does:**
• Maintains charge during long repairs
• Prevents memory loss
• Keeps modules powered
• Avoids relearns

**When Needed:**
• Multi-day repairs
• Repeated scanning/programming
• Interior work (lights on)
• Module programming
• Calibration procedures

**Methods:**
• Battery maintainer/charger
• Memory saver (during disconnect)
• External power supply

**Why Important:**
• Modules lose programming if battery dies
• Radio presets lost
• Seat/mirror positions reset
• Some systems need relearning
• Window auto-up/down reset

**Labor:** Typically 0.2-0.3 hours to set up

**Select YES when:**
• Repair over 3+ days
• Multiple programming steps
• Customer concern about settings
• Known parasitic drain issues

entries.question: Where do I start with a new estimate?
entries.answer:
**Got a new car? Here's how to start with MET:**

**Step 1: Start with SOP List** (ALWAYS first!)
• Vehicle type: Gas, Hybrid, or EV?
• Battery: Single or Dual?
• ADAS equipped? (cameras, sensors)
• Scan type for diagnostics
• Gateway unlock needed? (FCA 2018+)

**Step 2: Assess the Damage**
Look at the vehicle and decide which tabs apply:
• Panels damaged? → **Part Operations**
• Need paint? → **Cover Car** + **Refinish Operations**
• Frame/structural? → **Body Operations**
• Suspension hit? → **Mechanical Operations**
• Airbags deployed? → **SRS Operations**
• A/C damaged? → **Mechanical Operations**

**Step 3: Fill Out Each Tab**
• Work through inputs on each relevant tab
• Watch the outputs auto-calculate
• Labor hours and prices populate automatically

**Step 4: Review & Export**
• Check generated operations
• Copy to clipboard
• Export to CCC or Mitchell

**Pro Tips:**
• SOP List applies to EVERY single job
• Never skip battery disconnect/reconnect
• Always include pre-scan and post-scan
• Check ADAS on 2016+ vehicles
• FCA 2018+ needs Gateway unlock

entries.question: How do I know when and where to blend?
entries.answer:
**Blending Guidelines:**

**When Blending is NEEDED:**
• Metallic colors (most common)
• Pearl/Tri-coat colors (always)
• Aged/faded paint
• Color variance in repair
• High-visibility areas

**When Blending MAY NOT be needed:**
• Solid (non-metallic) colors
• New vehicles with fresh paint
• Hidden/non-visible areas
• Same batch paint available

**How Many Panels to Blend:**
• Minimum: 1 adjacent panel
• Metallic: 1-2 panels
• Pearl/Tri-coat: 2-3 panels
• Severe fade: May need more

**Blend Direction Rules:**
• Blend AWAY from damage
• Blend toward least visible area
• Horizontal surfaces: blend toward back
• Vertical surfaces: blend toward bottom

**Blend Panel Labor:**
• Typically 50-60% of full refinish
• Includes edge panel prep
• Clear coat extends further than color

**Pro Tips:**
• Let-down panel (spray out) before committing
• Document color variance with photos
• Check paint code/variant

entries.question: What is edging and when do I include it?
entries.answer:
**Edging (Jamb Refinish) Guide:**

**What is Edging?**
Painting the visible edges when doors/hood/trunk are open - areas NOT covered by standard exterior refinish.

**When to Include:**
• Door replacement (inner door edge)
• Fender replacement (A-pillar area)
• Quarter panel (trunk/tailgate opening)
• Hood (firewall edge if visible)
• Deck lid/tailgate (opening edge)

**Typical Edging Times:**
• Door edge: 0.3-0.5 hrs
• Fender A-pillar edge: 0.2-0.4 hrs
• Quarter opening edge: 0.4-0.6 hrs
• Hood edge: 0.2-0.3 hrs
• Deck lid edge: 0.2-0.4 hrs

**What's Involved:**
• Mask opening/jamb area
• Apply sealer if bare metal
• Color match jamb (may differ!)
• Clear coat
• Some jambs are textured

**Important Notes:**
• Jamb color may be DIFFERENT than exterior
• Check paint code for jamb variant
• NOT included in standard panel refinish
• Must be added separately

entries.question: How does clear coat work with blending?
entries.answer:
**Clear Coat & Blending:**

**Key Concept:**
Clear coat extends FURTHER than base coat (color) for invisible blend.

**Clear Coat Coverage:**
• Repaired panel: Full clear
• Blend panel: Full or partial clear
• Adjacent to blend: Clear may extend

**Clear Coat Blend Process:**
1. Base coat (color) blended first
2. Base coat stops mid-panel
3. Clear coat continues past base edge
4. Clear blends into existing clear
5. Buff blend line invisible

**Multi-Stage (Tri-coat/Pearl):**
• Base coat first
• Mid-coat (pearl/candy)
• Clear coat last
• Each layer blended differently

**Clear Coat Considerations:**
• Matte/flat clear - harder to blend
• OEM clear vs aftermarket
• UV protection matching
• Blend line buffing included?

**Labor Notes:**
• Clear coat time typically part of refinish hours
• Blend panel clear included in blend time
• Extra panels may be "clear only"
• Document if clear extends beyond blend

entries.question: What's included in bumper R&I labor?
entries.answer:
**Bumper R&I - Included vs Not Included:**

**INCLUDED in Bumper R&I:**
• Remove bumper cover fasteners
• Disconnect wiring (basic plugs)
• Remove and reinstall cover
• Basic bumper alignment
• Reinstall fasteners

**NOT INCLUDED (Add Separately):**
• Fog lights R&I
• Parking sensors R&I
• Radar sensors R&I
• Grille R&I (usually)
• Lower air deflector R&I
• Tow hook cover
• License plate R&I
• Impact bar/reinforcement R&I
• Energy absorber R&I
• Bumper brackets (if separate)
• Sensor recalibration
• Fog light aim

**Refinish NOT Included:**
• Adhesion promoter
• Flex additive
• Texture matching
• Scuff/prep inner surfaces

**P-Page Reference:**
Check CCC/Mitchell P-Pages for specific included/not included items - varies by estimating system!

entries.question: What's included in fender replace labor?
entries.answer:
**Fender Replace - Included vs Not Included:**

**INCLUDED in Fender Replace:**
• Remove/install fender bolts
• Disconnect basic wiring
• Transfer bolt-on parts (some systems)
• Basic gap alignment
• Install new fender

**NOT INCLUDED (Add Separately):**
• Headlight R&I (access) - DEBATED
• Wheel well liner R&I
• Side marker light R&I
• Antenna R&I
• Fender flare R&I
• Fender liner clips (replace)
• Seam sealer
• Corrosion protection
• Sound deadener
• Anti-chip coating
• Wheel alignment
• Structural measuring

**Refinish NOT Included:**
• Prep new unprimed part
• Prime & block
• Edging (jamb area)
• Undercoating inner fender
• Blend adjacent panels

**Pro Tip:**
Headlight R&I is often disputed - some insurers say it's included, CCC P-Pages say it IS included, but many shops add it. Document!

entries.question: What ADAS calibration is needed after windshield replacement?
entries.answer:
**Windshield ADAS Calibration:**

**Systems Potentially Affected:**
• Forward Collision Warning (FCW)
• Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)
• Lane Departure Warning (LDW)
• Lane Keep Assist (LKA)
• Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)
• Traffic Sign Recognition
• High Beam Assist
• Rain-sensing wipers
• Heads-up Display (HUD)

**Calibration Types:**

**Static Calibration:**
• Done in shop with targets
• Requires calibration equipment
• Specific distance/height requirements
• Level floor surface needed
• 1.0-2.0 hours typical

**Dynamic Calibration:**
• Requires test drive
• Specific speed requirements
• Weather/visibility conditions
• May take 15-30 minutes driving
• Some systems self-calibrate

**Always Required After:**
• Windshield replacement
• Windshield repair (in camera view)
• Camera removal/replacement
• Mirror removal (if camera attached)

**Labor:** $150-400 typical, depending on vehicle/system

**OEM Requirements:** Nearly ALL manufacturers require calibration - check position statements!

entries.question: What ADAS calibration is needed after bumper work?
entries.answer:
**Bumper ADAS Calibration Guide:**

**Front Bumper Systems:**
• Adaptive Cruise Control radar
• Automatic Emergency Braking
• Forward Collision Warning
• Parking sensors
• Cross-traffic alert (front)

**Rear Bumper Systems:**
• Parking sensors
• Backup camera
• Rear cross-traffic alert
• Rear automatic braking
• Trailer sensors

**When Calibration is Required:**
• Bumper cover R&I or replace
• Radar sensor R&I or replace
• Bumper reinforcement work
• Front-end structural repairs
• Wheel alignment (affects radar aim)

**Calibration Types:**

**Radar Calibration:**
• Static: Targets + scan tool
• Dynamic: Test drive
• Often BOTH required

**Parking Sensor Calibration:**
• Usually self-calibrating
• May need scan tool reset
• Verify function before delivery

**Backup Camera:**
• Usually no calibration
• May need angle adjustment
• Check overlay/guidelines

**Cost:** Front radar calibration typically $200-400

entries.question: Is ADAS calibration needed after wheel alignment?
entries.answer:
**Wheel Alignment & ADAS Calibration:**

**Short Answer:** Often YES!

**Why Alignment Affects ADAS:**
• ADAS sensors assume vehicle tracks straight
• Thrust angle changes affect sensor aim
• Lane keep uses wheel angle data
• Radar/camera pointed wrong direction

**Systems Affected:**
• Lane Departure Warning
• Lane Keep Assist
• Adaptive Cruise Control
• Forward Collision Warning
• Blind Spot Monitoring
• 360° Camera Systems

**OEM Requirements:**
• Many manufacturers REQUIRE calibration after alignment
• Check specific OEM position statements
• Toyota, Honda, GM, Ford all have requirements

**Best Practice:**
1. Pre-scan vehicle
2. Perform alignment
3. Perform ADAS calibration
4. Post-scan vehicle
5. Test drive to verify

**Alignment + Calibration Labor:**
• Alignment: Sublet $80-150
• ADAS calibration: $150-400
• May need both static AND dynamic

**Tip:** Bill ADAS calibration to insurance - it's required by OEM!

entries.question: Which vehicles need security gateway unlock?
entries.answer:
**Security Gateway Vehicles:**

entries.question: How do I justify scan fees to insurance?
entries.answer:
**Justifying Scan Fees:**

**Why Scans are Required:**
• OEM position statements mandate them
• Safety system verification
• Hidden damage discovery
• Liability protection
• Industry standard practice

**Documentation to Provide:**

**OEM Position Statements:**
• Nearly ALL manufacturers require scans
• Download from OEM repair sites
• Print and attach to supplement

**I-CAR References:**
• I-CAR scanning requirements
• Collision Repair News articles

**Specific Points:**
• "Pre-scan documents baseline"
• "Post-scan verifies safe repair"
• "OEM requires for warranty"
• "Shop liability without scan"

**If Denied:**
1. Request denial in writing
2. Cite OEM position statement
3. Reference I-CAR guidelines
4. Escalate to supervisor
5. Involve vehicle owner
6. Document everything

**Common Insurer Responses:**
• "Not needed" → OEM says otherwise
• "Included" → Not in P-Pages
• "We don't pay for that" → Check policy

**Labor:** 0.3-0.5 hrs OR flat rate ($50-100 each scan)

entries.question: How do I justify corrosion protection to insurance?
entries.answer:
**Justifying Corrosion Protection:**

**Why It's Required:**
• OEM procedures mandate it
• Prevents rust/warranty issues
• Restores factory protection
• NOT included in labor times
• Industry standard practice

**Types to Include:**
• Weld-through primer (0.2-0.3 hrs per area)
• Cavity wax (0.3-0.5 hrs per cavity)
• Seam sealer (0.3-0.5 hrs per seam)
• E-coat repair (0.4-0.6 hrs)
• Undercoating (0.3-0.5 hrs)

**Documentation:**

**P-Pages State:**
• "Corrosion protection NOT included"
• Must be added as separate operation

**OEM Procedures Show:**
• Where protection is required
• What products to use
• Print and attach to estimate

**I-CAR Standards:**
• Corrosion protection best practices
• Required for proper repair

**Negotiation Points:**
• "OEM procedure requires it"
• "Not included per P-Pages"
• "Rust claim prevention"
• "Factory protection must be restored"

**If Denied:**
• Provide OEM procedure printout
• Show P-Page excerpt
• Request written denial
• Escalate with documentation

entries.question: How do I justify blend panels to insurance?
entries.answer:
**Justifying Blend Panels:**

**Why Blending is Required:**
• Color match cannot be guaranteed
• Paint ages/fades over time
• Metallic/pearl shows mismatch
• Industry standard practice
• Customer satisfaction

**When to Blend:**
• Metallic colors (almost always)
• Pearl/tri-coat (always)
• Any visible mismatch
• Adjacent to repaired panel

**Documentation:**

**Color Variance Evidence:**
• Photos of color difference
• Spray-out card comparison
• Paint code verification

**Industry Standards:**
• I-CAR refinish guidelines
• Paint manufacturer recommendations
• OEM color matching procedures

**Negotiation Points:**
• "Color cannot be perfectly matched"
• "Metallic/pearl requires blending"
• "Industry standard practice"
• "Customer will see line"
• "Come-back prevention"

**Blend Time:**
• Typically 50-60% of full refinish
• Includes prep, base, clear
• May need multiple panels

**If Insurer Says "Try Without Blend":**
• Document their request
• Photo evidence if mismatch occurs
• Supplement for blend after
• Never deliver with visible line

entries.question: What's typical labor time for fender work?
entries.answer:
