entries.question: What are common supplement items?
entries.answer:
**Common Supplement Items:**

**Behind Bumper:**
• Impact bar/reinforcement
• Energy absorber (foam/honeycomb)
• Bumper brackets
• Sensor wiring
• AC condenser
• Radiator support damage

**Behind Fenders:**
• Inner fender/apron damage
• Wiring harness damage
• Wheel well rust/damage
• Core support damage
• Headlight brackets

**Behind Doors:**
• Intrusion beam damage
• Wiring damage
• Hinge/pillar damage
• Weatherstrip damage
• Rocker panel damage

**Structural (After Teardown):**
• Rail damage extent
• Floor pan damage
• Pillar damage
• Rocker inner
• Unibody cracks

**Suspension/Mechanical:**
• Control arm damage
• Subframe damage
• Steering component damage
• Engine cradle
• Brake line damage

**Refinish:**
• Additional blend panels
• Prior paint defects
• Extra prep needed
• Tri-coat discovered

**Best Practice:**
Photograph EVERYTHING during teardown!

entries.question: How do I properly document a supplement?
entries.answer:
**Supplement Documentation Best Practices:**

**Photo Requirements:**
• Overall shot showing context
• Close-up of specific damage
• Comparison to undamaged side
• Measuring tape in photo
• VIN tag in at least one photo
• Date/time stamp if possible

**Written Documentation:**
• When damage was discovered
• What was removed to reveal it
• Why it wasn't visible initially
• How it relates to impact

**Measurement Documentation:**
• Pre-repair measurements
• Dimension differences
• Structural spec comparison
• Photo of measuring system output

**Supplement Process:**
1. STOP work when damage found
2. Take extensive photos
3. Document in writing
4. Add operations to estimate
5. Contact adjuster/insurer
6. Wait for approval before proceeding
7. Get written approval

**Communication:**
• Email creates paper trail
• Include photos in correspondence
• Reference estimate line numbers
• Be specific about what and why

**Pro Tip:** Never assume approval - get it in writing before additional work!

entries.question: What are EV/Hybrid repair safety requirements?
entries.answer:
**EV/Hybrid Safety Requirements:**

**STOP - Before Any Work:**
• Is the vehicle powered down?
• Is high-voltage system disabled?
• Are you trained for EV/Hybrid?
• Do you have proper PPE?

**High-Voltage Identification:**
• Orange cables = HIGH VOLTAGE
• Never touch orange components
• HV battery usually under floor or rear
• Can be 400-800+ volts!

**Disable Procedures:**
• Follow OEM-specific procedure
• May require scan tool
• May require physical disconnect
• Wait time after disable (capacitors)

**Required PPE:**
• Class 0 electrical gloves (1000V rated)
• Safety glasses
• Non-conductive tools
• Insulated floor mat

**Additional Operations:**
• HV system disable: 0.5-1.0 hrs
• HV system re-enable: 0.3-0.5 hrs
• HV system verification: 0.2-0.3 hrs
• May need HV specialist

**Shop Requirements:**
• Trained technicians
• Proper equipment
• Emergency procedures
• Some shops NOT equipped for EV

**Labor Category:** Often higher rate for HV work

entries.question: What's different about Tesla repairs?
entries.answer:
**Tesla-Specific Considerations:**

**Parts:**
• OEM parts ONLY (Tesla requirement)
• Limited aftermarket availability
• Parts ordering through Tesla
• Some parts restricted to certified shops

**Scanning:**
• Tesla-specific diagnostic required
• Tesla Toolbox or certified scan tool
• Higher scan fees typical
• OTA updates may affect systems

**Calibration:**
• Autopilot cameras (up to 8)
• Ultrasonic sensors (up to 12)
• Requires Tesla equipment
• May need Tesla Mobile Service

**Structural:**
• Aluminum construction (most models)
• Dedicated aluminum area required
• Specific riveting equipment
• Adhesive bonding
• No heat straightening aluminum

**Battery Considerations:**
• Large HV battery pack
• Special handling/storage
• Battery damage = possible total loss
• Fire risk protocols

**Certifications:**
• Tesla Certified Body Shop preferred
• Training requirements
• Equipment requirements
• Not all shops qualified

**Labor Rates:**
• Often commands premium
• Specialized training required

entries.question: What's different about truck/pickup repairs?
entries.answer:
**Truck/Pickup Specific Operations:**

**Bed Side Panel:**
• Often requires cab-off or bed-off
• Cab-off labor: 6.0-12.0 hrs
• Bed-off labor: 3.0-6.0 hrs
• Sectioning common
• Inner wheelhouse access

**Cab Corner:**
• High rust area
• Sectioning procedures
• Inner cab access needed
• Check for hidden rust

**Frame Considerations:**
• Body-on-frame construction
• Frame damage = frame machine
• Frame section/replace possible
• Frame labor separate from body
• Boxed vs C-channel frames

**Tailgate:**
• Aluminum on many new trucks
• Integrated cameras/sensors
• Power tailgate systems
• Expensive to replace

**Truck-Specific Items:**
• Bed liner R&I
• Tonneau cover R&I
• Fifth wheel hitch
• Gooseneck hitch
• Toolbox R&I
• Bedside steps/rails
• Tow mirrors

**Diesel Considerations:**
• Dual battery systems
• DEF system (damage?)
• Turbo/intercooler
• Different scan requirements

entries.question: What's different about aluminum panel repairs?
entries.answer:
**Aluminum-Specific Requirements:**

**Dedicated Area Required:**
• Separate from steel work
• Own set of tools
• Clean environment
• No steel particles!

**Why Separation Matters:**
• Galvanic corrosion risk
• Steel contamination causes rust
• Embedded particles = warranty issues

**Tools Required:**
• Aluminum-specific hammers
• Aluminum dollies
• Dedicated grinder/sander
• Different filler products
• Specific sandpaper

**Joining Methods:**
• MIG welding (aluminum wire)
• Rivet bonding
• Self-piercing rivets
• Flow-form rivets
• Structural adhesives
• NO traditional spot welding

**Repair Limitations:**
• Less stretchable than steel
• Heat affects strength
• Often replace vs repair
• OEM procedures strict

**Common Aluminum Vehicles:**
• Ford F-150 (2015+)
• Audi (many models)
• Jaguar/Land Rover
• Tesla (all models)
• Corvette (structural)
• Many luxury brands

**Labor:**
• Often higher rate for aluminum
• More time for careful work

entries.question: Where do I find the paint code?
entries.answer:
**Paint Code Locations by Make:**

**Common Locations:**
• Driver door jamb (most vehicles)
• Driver B-pillar
• Under hood (firewall)
• Glove box
• Trunk lid jamb

**By Manufacturer:**
• **Ford**: Driver door jamb
• **GM**: Driver door jamb or glove box
• **Chrysler/Stellantis**: Driver door jamb
• **Toyota**: Driver door jamb
• **Honda**: Driver door jamb
• **BMW**: Under hood or door jamb
• **Mercedes**: Driver door jamb
• **Tesla**: Driver B-pillar

**Paint Code Format:**
• Usually 2-4 characters
• May have variant code (interior/exterior)
• Example: WA8555 (GM), PW7 (Chrysler)

**Tri-Coat/Pearl Identification:**
• Code usually indicates multi-stage
• Check paint manufacturer cross-reference
• Look for "Tri" or "3-Stage" notation

**If Code is Damaged:**
• Check VIN decoder
• Contact dealer with VIN
• Use spectrophotometer to match

entries.question: When do I need structural measuring?
entries.answer:
**When Structural Measuring is Required:**

**Always Measure:**
• Any unibody damage
• Frame rail damage (BOF)
• Suspension/steering damage
• Collision over 10-15 mph
• Airbag deployment
• Major panel replacement
• Customer complaint of pulling/drift

**Measuring Points:**
• Strut/shock towers
• Control arm mounts
• Subframe mounting
• Radiator support
• Frame rails (all points)
• Cowl/windshield opening
• Door openings
• Roof rail/pillars

**Equipment Types:**
• Computerized systems (Car-O-Liner, Chief, etc.)
• Laser systems
• Tram gauge (basic)
• Centering gauges

**Labor:**
• Setup/measure: 0.5-1.5 hrs
• Document: 0.2-0.3 hrs
• Per-point additional: varies
• Blueprint: 1.0-3.0 hrs

**Documentation:**
• Print before/after specs
• Include in repair file
• Photo measuring equipment output
• Attach to estimate

**Note:** NOT included in panel replacement labor!

entries.question: What are the sectioning guidelines?
entries.answer:
**Panel Sectioning Guidelines:**

**What is Sectioning?**
Cutting and replacing only PART of a panel instead of full replacement.

**When to Section:**
• Damage localized to one area
• OEM allows sectioning
• More cost-effective than full panel
• Service part available

**OEM Sectioning Rules:**
• ALWAYS check OEM procedures first!
• Approved cut locations only
• Some panels cannot be sectioned
• Specific joint types required

**Common Sectioning Areas:**
• Quarter panel (at door opening)
• Rocker panel (at door openings)
• Frame rails (approved locations)
• A/B/C pillars (approved only)
• Roof at pillars

**Sectioning Requirements:**
• Offset joints (not straight across)
• Proper joint type (lap, butt, etc.)
• Weld-through primer
• Seam sealer
• Corrosion protection

**Labor:**
• Sectioning typically MORE labor than full replace
• Includes cutting, fitting, welding
• Add corrosion protection
• Add seam sealer

**Do NOT Section:**
• High-strength steel (UHSS, AHSS) - often
• Aluminum panels (usually)
• Without OEM approval

entries.question: What is DEG and how do I use it?
entries.answer:
**DEG (Database Enhancement Gateway):**

**What is DEG?**
• Industry organization for database accuracy
• Files inquiries on what IS/ISN'T included
• Creates rulings on disputed operations
• Free resource for shops

**Website:** DEGweb.org

**How to Use DEG:**
1. Search existing inquiries first
2. Find ruling on your disputed item
3. Print ruling for documentation
4. Submit new inquiry if none exists

**Common DEG Topics:**
• Included/not included operations
• Labor time disputes
• Material definitions
• Procedure clarifications
• R&I operations

**Example Inquiry Types:**
• "Is R&I headlight included in fender replace?"
• "Is corrosion protection included?"
• "What does 'adjacent' mean for overlap?"

**Using DEG in Negotiations:**
• Print relevant inquiry/ruling
• Attach to estimate/supplement
• Reference inquiry number
• Shows industry position

**DEG is FREE** to search and use!

**Pro Tip:** Search DEG BEFORE arguing with adjuster - know the ruling first.

entries.question: How do I handle prior damage?
entries.answer:
**Handling Prior/Pre-Existing Damage:**

**Documentation is KEY:**
• Photo ALL damage at intake
• Note location and severity
• Measure if possible
• Get customer acknowledgment

**On the Estimate:**
• Clearly note "Prior damage - unrelated"
• Separate line items if affected
• Don't include in repair scope
• May need deduction if panel replaced

**Prior Damage Scenarios:**

**Prior Dent on Replacement Panel:**
• Not repair responsibility
• Customer pays difference if they want fixed
• Note on delivery docs

**Prior Rust:**
• Not collision-related
• May affect repair quality
• Disclose to customer/insurer

**Previous Poor Repair:**
• Document with photos
• May complicate current repair
• Additional labor if affects work
• Disclose immediately

**Customer Communication:**
• Point out at drop-off
• Have them acknowledge
• Include in final QC

**Protect Yourself:**
• "As-is" for prior damage
• Written acknowledgment
• Photos in file
• Clear on delivery form

entries.question: What do I do when aftermarket parts don't fit?
entries.answer:
**Aftermarket Parts Fit Issues:**

**Document BEFORE Installing:**
• Photo new part vs old part
• Measure critical dimensions
• Note any visible defects
• Compare mounting holes

**Common Fit Problems:**
• Mounting holes misaligned
• Panel contour wrong
• Gap issues
• Attachment points don't line up
• Finish quality poor

**What to Do:**
1. Document with photos/measurements
2. Contact parts supplier
3. Request return authorization
4. Write supplement for OEM part
5. Include fit time if adjustable

**Supplement Language:**
• "A/M part does not fit properly"
• "Mounting holes misaligned by Xmm"
• "Requesting OEM due to fit issues"
• Include photos!

**Fit/Adjust Time:**
• If part CAN fit with work: 0.5-2.0 hrs
• Document extra effort required
• Not always payable by insurer

**Customer Rights:**
• Can request OEM at any time
• May pay price difference
• Check state laws on aftermarket

**Keep the A/M Part** until resolution!

entries.question: When do I include AC evacuate and recharge?
entries.answer:
**AC Evacuate & Recharge:**

**When Required:**
• Condenser R&I or replace
• Radiator R&I or replace (some vehicles)
• Compressor R&I
• Any AC line disconnect
• Core support replacement
• Front structural repairs
• Evaporator access

**What It Includes:**
• Recover existing refrigerant
• Evacuate system (vacuum)
• Check for leaks
• Recharge with proper amount
• Add oil if required
• Verify operation

**Labor:**
• Evacuate/Recharge: 0.8-1.5 hrs
• Often sublet to specialty shop
• Typical sublet: $80-150

**Materials:**
• Refrigerant (R134a or R1234yf)
• R1234yf is expensive ($50-100+ per lb)
• PAG oil if needed
• Dye for leak detection

**Documentation:**
• Before: Amount recovered
• After: Amount recharged
• Note any leaks found
• Operating pressures

**Note:** R1234yf (newer vehicles) costs significantly more than R134a!

entries.question: When do I need to include wheel alignment?
entries.answer:
**When Wheel Alignment is Required:**

**Always Include:**
• Any suspension component replacement
• Steering component replacement
• Subframe R&I or replacement
• Frame/structural straightening
• Strut tower repair
• Control arm replacement
• Ball joint replacement
• Tie rod replacement
• Knuckle/spindle replacement

**Usually Include:**
• Quarter panel replacement (may shift specs)
• Rocker panel replacement
• Major rear collision (thrust angle)
• Wheel/tire replacement
• Cradle/crossmember work

**Types:**
• **Front alignment**: Front suspension work
• **Thrust angle**: Rear suspension affects front
• **4-wheel alignment**: Best practice for most repairs

**Labor:**
• Typically sublet: $80-150
• 4-wheel alignment: $100-180
• Specialty vehicles: more

**ADAS Note:**
• Alignment BEFORE calibration
• Alignment affects ADAS aiming
• May need re-calibration if alignment changes

**Customer Safety:**
• Never skip alignment after suspension work
• Tire wear and handling affected
• Document if customer declines

entries.question: When do I include headlight aim?
entries.answer:
**Headlight Aim/Adjustment:**

**When Required:**
• Headlight replacement
• Headlight R&I
• Front structural repairs
• Fender replacement (some vehicles)
• Hood replacement (some vehicles)
• Suspension repair (front)
• After collision in headlight area

**Types of Systems:**

**Manual Aim:**
• Adjustment screws on headlight
• Aim to specifications
• Check both vertical and horizontal

**Auto-Leveling (HID/LED):**
• Requires scan tool initialization
• Sensor calibration may be needed
• Level vehicle first (tire pressure!)

**Adaptive Headlights:**
• May require calibration
• Steering angle sensor related
• Check OEM procedures

**Labor:**
• Manual aim: 0.3-0.5 hrs
• Auto-leveling reset: 0.2-0.3 hrs
• Adaptive calibration: 0.5-1.0 hrs

**Equipment:**
• Headlight aiming machine/screen
• Level surface required
• Scan tool for auto-leveling

**Safety:** Improperly aimed headlights = safety hazard!

entries.question: When do I include sound deadener?
entries.answer:
**Sound Deadener Replacement:**

**What is it?**
• Material applied to panels to reduce noise/vibration
• Often tar-like or foam sheets
• Factory applied at specific locations

**When to Include:**
• Panel replacement (floor, quarter, roof)
• Door skin replacement
• Trunk floor repair/replace
• Roof panel replacement
• Any area with factory deadener

**Common Locations:**
• Floor pan (entire surface)
• Door inner panels
• Trunk floor
• Roof panel
• Quarter panel inner
• Firewall

**Types:**
• Butyl sheets (peel and stick)
• Spray-on coating
• Foam sheets
• Factory specific materials

**Labor:**
• Application: 0.3-0.8 hrs per panel
• Depends on coverage area
• Removal for repair: additional time

**Materials:**
• NOT included in labor
• Separate material charge
• Match factory type if possible

**NOT Included** in panel replacement labor - add separately!

entries.question: When do I include undercoating?
entries.answer:
**Undercoating/Chip Coating:**

**Types:**

**Undercoating:**
• Applied to underbody
• Protects from road debris
• Rubberized coating

**Anti-Chip Coating:**
• Applied to rocker panels
• Lower doors, fenders
• Quarter panel lower
• Protects from stone chips

**When to Include:**
• Any replacement panel with factory coating
• Rocker panel replacement
• Fender replacement (lower edge)
• Door replacement (lower edge)
• Quarter panel (lower/wheel well)
• Floor pan repair/replacement

**Application:**
• Clean surface first
• Apply per manufacturer specs
• Match factory texture
• May require special equipment

**Labor:**
• Application: 0.2-0.5 hrs per area
• Full underbody: 1.0-2.0 hrs
• Texture matching: additional

**Materials:**
• Rubberized undercoating
• Chip-resistant coating
• NOT included in labor

**Refinish Category** for most applications

**Note:** Check OEM specs for correct product type!

entries.question: What are the body filler guidelines?
entries.answer:
**Body Filler Guidelines:**

**Maximum Depth:**
• Generally 1/4" (6mm) maximum
• Over bare, properly prepared metal
• Less on flexible areas
• OEM may specify limits

**When Filler is Appropriate:**
• Minor dings and dents after metal work
• Weld grind areas
• Small imperfections
• Skim coating for final finish

**When NOT to Use Filler:**
• Deep damage (replace or more metal work)
• Structural areas
• Over rust or corrosion
• Over paint (adhesion issues)
• To "hide" improper repair

**Proper Application:**
1. Bare, clean metal only
2. Grind/sand for adhesion
3. Apply in thin layers
4. Allow proper cure time
5. Block sand to contour

**Filler Types:**
• Standard polyester filler
• Premium/lightweight filler
• Finishing/glazing putty
• Aluminum filler (specialty)

**Labor:**
• Included in repair labor
• Heavy filling = more time
• Block/sand = more time

**Quality Check:**
• Use magnetic gauge to measure depth
• Feel for contour match
• Prime and check before color

entries.question: What if the color doesn't match?
entries.answer:
**Color Matching Issues:**

**Common Causes:**
• Wrong paint code
• Wrong variant
• Aged/faded original paint
• Metallic orientation
• Application technique
• Environmental factors

**Steps to Solve:**

**1. Verify Paint Code:**
• Double-check code on vehicle
• Check VIN decoder
• Verify variant if applicable

**2. Check Spray-Out:**
• Always do let-down panel first
• Compare to vehicle in same light
• Check multiple angles (metallic)

**3. Tinting Options:**
• Adjust formula per paint system
• Add/reduce tints as needed
• Document adjustments made

**4. Blending:**
• Most reliable solution
• Blend into adjacent panels
• Clear coat further than base

**Labor for Tinting:**
• Color adjust: 0.3-0.5 hrs
• Multiple attempts: more time
• Premium colors: more time

**Document Everything:**
• Photos in different lighting
• Note tinting adjustments
• Customer sign-off

**Some Colors are Difficult:**
• Tri-coat/pearl
• Metallic with flop
• Candy colors
• Older faded vehicles

entries.question: What are OEM position statements?
entries.answer:
**OEM Position Statements:**

**What They Are:**
• Official manufacturer statements on repair procedures
• Define required operations
• Specify equipment/training needs
• Liability guidance

**Common Topics:**
• Pre/post repair scanning
• ADAS calibration requirements
• Structural repair limitations
• Parts requirements (OEM vs A/M)
• Sectioning approvals
• Welding specifications

**Where to Find Them:**
• OEM repair procedure sites
• I-CAR RTS portal
• OEM1Stop.com
• Manufacturer websites
• Collision Hub

**How to Use:**
• Print and attach to estimate
• Reference in supplement
• Support disputed operations
• Liability documentation

**Key Manufacturers with Statements:**
• Honda/Acura - comprehensive
• Toyota/Lexus - detailed
• GM - extensive
• Ford - growing library
• FCA/Stellantis - available
• BMW, Mercedes, Audi - strict

**Example Uses:**
• "OEM requires pre/post scan"
• "OEM prohibits aftermarket structural parts"
• "OEM requires ADAS calibration"

**Pro Tip:** Download and save relevant statements to your shop library!

entries.question: How does rental/loss of use work?
entries.answer:
**Rental Car & Loss of Use:**

**Rental Coverage:**
• Part of customer's policy (optional)
• Daily limit (typically $30-50/day)
• Maximum days (often 30 days)
• Check policy for specifics

**Loss of Use (No Coverage):**
• Customer may claim actual loss
• Reasonable daily rate
• Only for repair duration
• Third-party claims

**Shop Responsibilities:**
• Repair in timely manner
• Communicate delays promptly
• Document parts delays
• Don't extend unnecessarily

**When Rental Starts:**
• Usually when repairs begin
• May start at drop-off (policy dependent)
• Authorize with insurer first

**Rental Considerations:**
• Comparable vehicle
• Like kind and quality
• May upgrade at customer expense

**Delay Documentation:**
• Parts on backorder (document!)
• Waiting for authorization
• Supplement delays
• Weather delays

**Who Pays for Delays:**
• Shop delay: Shop responsibility
• Parts delay: Insurance typically
• Authorization delay: Insurance
• Customer delay: Customer

**Pro Tip:** Communicate proactively about timeline!

entries.question: How do I handle tow and storage charges?
entries.answer:
**Tow & Storage Charges:**

**Towing:**
• From accident scene to shop
• Typically covered by insurance
• Document mileage and type
• Flatbed vs wheel-lift

**Typical Tow Charges:**
• Hook-up fee: $75-150
• Per mile: $3-8/mile
• After hours: premium
• Specialty (lowered, AWD): more

**Storage:**
• Daily rate for vehicle on premises
• Starts when vehicle arrives
• Ends when customer picks up

**Typical Storage Rates:**
• Inside: $35-75/day
• Outside: $25-50/day
• Varies by region

**When Storage is Charged:**
• Vehicle awaiting authorization
• Total loss pending pickup
• Parts delay (debatable)
• Customer delay in pickup

**Insurance Considerations:**
• Most policies have limits
• May dispute excessive storage
• Document authorization delays
• Total loss: storage until title transfer

**Documentation:**
• Date/time received
• Daily log if long term
• Photos of vehicle storage
• Notice to customer/insurer

**State Regulations:**
• Some states limit storage rates
• Lien laws vary
• Check local requirements

entries.question: What administrative fees can I charge?
entries.answer:
**Administrative & Miscellaneous Fees:**

**Common Charges:**

**Shop Supplies/Materials:**
• Consumables not in specific operations
• Tape, paper, cleaners, etc.
• Often % of labor (3-10%)
• Or flat rate per job

**Hazardous Waste Disposal:**
• Paint waste, solvents
• Refrigerant recovery
• Oil/fluid disposal
• Environmental compliance

**Administrative Fees:**
• File handling
• Documentation
• Photo processing
• Storage of records

**Often Disputed Items:**
• Shop supplies (insurers resist)
• Administrative fees (often denied)
• Environmental fees (varies)

**What's Typically Accepted:**
• Hazmat disposal (documented)
• Specific supplies on estimate
• Storage (reasonable rate)
• Towing (actual invoice)

**Best Practices:**
• Itemize when possible
• Support with documentation
• Know insurer guidelines
• Be reasonable

**State Laws:**
• Some states allow shop supplies
• Rates may be regulated
• Check local requirements

**Customer Pay:**
• Can charge more than insurance
• Disclose upfront
• Get authorization

entries.question: When do I include diagnostic time?
entries.answer:
**Diagnostic Time:**

**When to Include:**
• Electrical problems beyond obvious damage
• Intermittent issues
• Multiple possible causes
• System malfunction after repair
• Check engine/warning lights
• ADAS malfunction

**What Diagnostic Time Covers:**
• Analyzing symptoms
• Using diagnostic equipment
• Testing circuits/components
• Researching technical info
• Isolating problem source

**Typical Diagnostic Labor:**
• Basic: 0.5-1.0 hrs
• Moderate: 1.0-2.0 hrs
• Complex: 2.0-4.0+ hrs
• Depends on issue complexity

**Pre/Post Scan vs Diagnostic:**
• **Scan**: Read/clear codes, verify systems
• **Diagnostic**: Determine WHY code exists
• Different operations!

**Documentation:**
• Note symptoms
• List tests performed
• Document findings
• Time spent

**Disputes:**
• Insurers may question extensive diag
• Document your process
• Show why time was needed
• Keep detailed notes

**Labor Category:** Usually Mechanical (M)

entries.question: Can I charge for teardown/disassembly?
entries.answer:
**Teardown/Disassembly Charges:**

**When Teardown is Needed:**
• Hidden damage suspected
• Accurate estimate required
• Blueprint process
• Insurance requested detailed estimate

**Types of Teardown:**

**Partial Teardown:**
• Remove damaged panels
• Access hidden areas
• Limited disassembly
• 1.0-3.0 hrs typical

**Full Blueprint:**
• Complete disassembly
• All damage exposed
• Comprehensive estimate
• 3.0-8.0+ hrs

**Charging for Teardown:**
• If repairs done: Usually absorbed into repair labor
• If customer declines repair: May charge teardown
• If total loss: May be separate charge
• Get authorization BEFORE teardown

**Customer Pay Scenarios:**
• Looking at buying used car
• Second opinion
• Declines repair after teardown
• Not using shop for repair

**Insurance Scenarios:**
• May pay for blueprinting
• Often disputes if excessive
• Total loss may include teardown

**Best Practice:**
• Written authorization
• Clear about charges
• Estimate teardown time
• Document with photos

entries.question: How do I charge for clips and fasteners?
entries.answer:
**Clips & Fasteners:**

**Types:**
• Push-pin retainers
• Panel clips
• Fender liner clips
• Trim fasteners
• Bumper clips
• Wiring harness clips
• Weatherstrip retainers

**When to Replace:**
• Broken during R&I
• One-time use (OEM spec)
• Damaged/worn
• Missing

**Charging Guidelines:**

**Included:**
• Clips that come with new part
• Some basic fasteners with R&I

**Not Included (Charge Separately):**
• Broken clips during R&I
• Clips not included with part
• One-time-use fasteners
• Hardware kits

**Pricing:**
• Individual clips: $0.50-5.00 each
• Hardware kits: $10-50+
• OEM vs aftermarket available

**Documentation:**
• Count broken clips
• Note on estimate
• Photo if disputed

**Labor Note:**
• Time to replace clips usually minimal
• May need to order (adds time)
• Some clips dealer-only

**Pro Tip:** Keep common clips in stock to avoid delays!

entries.question: How do I estimate wiring/harness repairs?
entries.answer:
**Wiring & Harness Repairs:**

**Types of Damage:**
• Cut/severed wires
• Crushed harness
• Melted insulation
• Corroded connectors
• Pulled connections
• Chafed wiring

**Repair Options:**

**Splice Repair:**
• Individual wire repair
• Solder or crimp connection
• Heat shrink protection
• 0.3-0.5 hrs per splice

**Connector Repair:**
• Replace terminal/pin
• New connector install
• Pigtail replacement
• 0.3-1.0 hrs per connector

**Harness Section Replace:**
• Replace damaged section
• Splice at both ends
• Match wire gauge/type
• 1.0-3.0+ hrs

**Full Harness Replace:**
• Complete harness replacement
• Major labor (many hours)
• Often structural disassembly
• Expensive option

**When to Repair vs Replace:**
• Few wires: Repair
• Multiple areas: Consider replace
• Critical systems: May need replace
• OEM may prohibit repairs

**Labor Category:** Mechanical (M) typically

**Materials:**
• Connectors, terminals
• Wire, solder, shrink tube
• OEM repair kits available

entries.question: What about glass operations?
entries.answer:
**Glass Operations:**

**Types:**
• Windshield (laminated)
• Door glass (tempered)
• Quarter glass (fixed or movable)
• Back glass (tempered, may be heated)
• Sunroof/moonroof glass

**Windshield:**
• Usually sublet to glass company
• Typical cost: $200-500+
• OEM vs aftermarket option
• Calibration if ADAS equipped!

**Door Glass:**
• Often shop can do
• R&I involves regulator
• 1.0-2.0 hrs typical
• Part cost varies

**Fixed Glass (Quarter, Back):**
• Bonded/sealed in place
• More labor to replace
• May require trim removal
• 1.0-3.0 hrs

**Calibration Requirements:**
• Windshield with camera: YES
• Must calibrate after replacement
• Static and/or dynamic
• Add $150-400

**Labor Considerations:**
• Moulding replacement
• Seal/urethane
• Clips and hardware
• Trim R&I for access

**Sublet vs In-House:**
• Windshield: Usually sublet
• Side glass: Can be in-house
• Mobile glass service available

**Insurance:**
• Glass claims often separate
• Deductible may be waived
• Check policy specifics

entries.question: What's involved in mirror replacement?
entries.answer:
**Mirror Operations:**

**Mirror Types:**
• Manual (basic)
• Power adjust
• Heated
• Turn signal integrated
• Blind spot monitoring
• Camera integrated (360°)
• Auto-dimming
• Memory position

**Replacement Options:**

**Mirror Glass Only:**
• Just the reflective element
• Snaps or adheres in place
• 0.2-0.5 hrs
• $20-100 part cost

**Mirror Assembly:**
• Complete mirror unit
• More common for damage
• 0.5-1.5 hrs
• $100-800+ part cost

**Components:**
• Housing/cover
• Mirror glass
• Motor (power)
• Heating element
• Turn signal module
• BSM sensor
• Camera

**Special Considerations:**

**Blind Spot Monitor:**
• Calibration required!
• $100-200 calibration
• Cannot skip if equipped

**Camera Equipped:**
• 360° camera calibration
• Or camera R&I to transfer

**Wiring:**
• Check harness routing
• Connector compatibility
• May need adapter

**Paint:**
• Mirror cap often painted
• Add refinish time if needed

entries.question: What is the SCRS Blend Study?
entries.answer:
**SCRS Blend Study (2022)**

A landmark study by the Society of Collision Repair Specialists that challenged industry blend formulas.

**The Problem:**
• All 3 estimating systems (CCC/MOTOR, Mitchell, Audatex) calculated blend time as **50% of full refinish**
• Industry assumed blending takes LESS work than full panel refinish

**The Study:**
• 5 major paint manufacturers: AkzoNobel, Axalta, BASF, PPG, Sherwin-Williams
• Conducted at GFS Center for Excellence (Wisconsin)
• Used 2018 Ford F-150 parts (hoods, fenders, doors)
• DEKRA served as third-party auditor

**Key Finding:**
• Blend time was actually **31.59% GREATER** than full refinish!
• NOT 50% less as databases calculated

**Quote from Aaron Schulenburg (SCRS):**
"These results very clearly establish that performing the processes necessary to blend a panel does not take less labor than the processes to fully refinish a panel."

**Industry Response:**
• CCC/MOTOR and Audatex made changes to their systems
• Mitchell announced enhancements to Mitchell Cloud Estimating

**Full Report:** scrs.com/blendstudy

**Use this to support blend time on estimates!**

entries.question: How does blend refinish work?
entries.answer:
**Blend Refinish Operations:**

**Definition:**
Blending is the application of color to a portion of an undamaged adjacent panel to facilitate color match, followed by clear coat over the entire blended panel.

**When to Blend:**
• Adjacent to refinished panel
• Color match concerns (metallic, pearl, tri-coat)
• Sun fading on vehicle
• OEM color variation

**Blend Process:**
1. Clean blend area
2. Scuff/sand blend zone
3. Mask non-spray areas
4. Apply color with fade at blend edge
5. Apply clear coat to ENTIRE panel
6. Allow flash time
7. Remove masking

**What's INCLUDED in Blend:**
• Partial masking
• Limited surface prep on blend area
• Color application with fade
• Clear coat application

**What's NOT INCLUDED in Blend:**
• Full panel prep
• Featheredging
• Primer application
• Prime and block

**SCRS Blend Study Finding:**
• Blend actually takes **31.59% MORE** time than full refinish
• NOT 50% less as databases show
• Use scrs.com/blendstudy to support your estimates

**P-Page Reference:** G36
